[Elephanta Caves](/wiki/Elephanta_Caves "Elephanta Caves"), [Chhatrapati Shivaji Terminus](/wiki/Chhatrapati_Shivaji_Terminus "Chhatrapati Shivaji Terminus"), and [The Victorian and Art Deco Ensemble of Mumbai](/wiki/The_Victorian_and_Art_Deco_Ensemble_of_Mumbai "The Victorian and Art Deco Ensemble of Mumbai")

[Mumbai Metro](/wiki/Mumbai_Metro "Mumbai Metro") and [Mumbai Monorail](/wiki/Mumbai_Monorail "Mumbai Monorail")  **[Commuter rail](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail")**: [Mumbai Suburban Railway](/wiki/Mumbai_Suburban_Railway "Mumbai Suburban Railway") |

([/mʊmˈbaɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*muum-BY*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); [Marathi](/wiki/Marathi_language "Marathi language"): *Mumbaī*, pronounced [[ˈmumbəi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Marathi "Help:IPA/Marathi") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Mumbai_Pronunciation.ogg "File:Mumbai Pronunciation.ogg")), also known as **Bombay** ([/bɒmˈbeɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*bom-BAY*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); its official name until 1995), is the [capital city](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city") of the [Indian](/wiki/India "India") [state](/wiki/States_and_union_territories_of_India "States and union territories of India") of [Maharashtra](/wiki/Maharashtra "Maharashtra"). Mumbai is the [financial capital](/wiki/Financial_centre "Financial centre") and the [most populous city proper](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_India_by_population "List of cities in India by population") of India, with an estimated population of 12.5 million (1.25 [crore](/wiki/Crore "Crore")).[[18]](#cite_note-Cities1Lakhandabove-18) Mumbai is the centre of the [Mumbai Metropolitan Region](/wiki/Mumbai_Metropolitan_Region "Mumbai Metropolitan Region"), which is among the [most populous metropolitan areas in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_cities "List of largest cities") with a population of over 23 million (2.3 crore).[[19]](#cite_note-19) Mumbai lies on the [Konkan](/wiki/Konkan "Konkan") coast on the west coast of India and has a deep [natural harbour](/wiki/Natural_harbour "Natural harbour"). In 2008, Mumbai was named an [alpha](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network#Alpha "Globalization and World Cities Research Network") [world city](/wiki/World_city "World city").[[20]](#cite_note-lboro2008-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) Mumbai has the [highest number of billionaires](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_number_of_billionaires "List of cities by number of billionaires") out of any city in [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia").[[a]](#cite_note-23)

**Mumbai** ([/mʊmˈbaɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*muum-BY*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); [Marathi](/wiki/Marathi_language "Marathi language"): *Mumbaī*, pronounced [[ˈmumbəi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Marathi "Help:IPA/Marathi") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Mumbai_Pronunciation.ogg "File:Mumbai Pronunciation.ogg")), also known as **Bombay** ([/bɒmˈbeɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*bom-BAY*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); its official name until 1995), is the [capital city](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city") of the [Indian](/wiki/India "India") [state](/wiki/States_and_union_territories_of_India "States and union territories of India") of [Maharashtra](/wiki/Maharashtra "Maharashtra"). Mumbai is the [financial capital](/wiki/Financial_centre "Financial centre") and the [most populous city proper](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_India_by_population "List of cities in India by population") of India, with an estimated population of 12.5 million (1.25 [crore](/wiki/Crore "Crore")).[[18]](#cite_note-Cities1Lakhandabove-18) Mumbai is the centre of the [Mumbai Metropolitan Region](/wiki/Mumbai_Metropolitan_Region "Mumbai Metropolitan Region"), which is among the [most populous metropolitan areas in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_cities "List of largest cities") with a population of over 23 million (2.3 crore).[[19]](#cite_note-19) Mumbai lies on the [Konkan](/wiki/Konkan "Konkan") coast on the west coast of India and has a deep [natural harbour](/wiki/Natural_harbour "Natural harbour"). In 2008, Mumbai was named an [alpha](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network#Alpha "Globalization and World Cities Research Network") [world city](/wiki/World_city "World city").[[20]](#cite_note-lboro2008-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) Mumbai has the [highest number of billionaires](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_number_of_billionaires "List of cities by number of billionaires") out of any city in [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia").[[a]](#cite_note-23)

The seven islands that constitute Mumbai were earlier home to communities of [Marathi language](/wiki/Marathi_language "Marathi language")-speaking [Koli people](/wiki/Koli_people "Koli people").[[23]](#cite_note-james_customs-24)[[24]](#cite_note-munshi-25)[[25]](#cite_note-rnmehta-26) For centuries, the [seven islands of Bombay](/wiki/Seven_islands_of_Bombay "Seven islands of Bombay") were under the control of successive [indigenous rulers](/wiki/History_of_Mumbai_under_indigenous_empires "History of Mumbai under indigenous empires") before being [ceded](/wiki/Cession "Cession") to the [Portuguese Empire](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire_in_the_East "Portuguese Empire in the East"), and subsequently to the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") in 1661, as part of the [dowry of Catherine of Braganza](/wiki/Catherine_of_Braganza#Marriage "Catherine of Braganza") in her marriage to [Charles II of England](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England").[[26]](#cite_note-27) Beginning in 1782, Mumbai was reshaped by the [Hornby Vellard](/wiki/Hornby_Vellard "Hornby Vellard") project,[[27]](#cite_note-Dwivedi_2001_28-28) which undertook [reclamation](/wiki/Land_reclamation "Land reclamation") of the area between the seven islands from the [Arabian Sea](/wiki/Arabian_Sea "Arabian Sea").[[28]](#cite_note-fp-onceuponatime-29) Along with the construction of major [roads](/wiki/Roads_in_India "Roads in India") and [railways](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_India "Rail transport in India"), the reclamation project, completed in 1845, transformed Mumbai into a major seaport on the [Arabian Sea](/wiki/Arabian_Sea "Arabian Sea"). Mumbai in the 19th century was characterised by economic and educational development. During the early 20th century, it became a strong base for the [Indian independence movement](/wiki/Indian_independence_movement "Indian independence movement"). Upon India's independence in 1947, the city was incorporated into [Bombay State](/wiki/Bombay_State "Bombay State"). In 1960, following the [Samyukta Maharashtra Movement](/wiki/Samyukta_Maharashtra_Movement "Samyukta Maharashtra Movement"), a new state of Maharashtra was created with Mumbai as the capital.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

Mumbai is the [financial](/wiki/Financial_centre "Financial centre"), commercial,[[30]](#cite_note-millionaireRoleModel-31) and [entertainment capital](/wiki/Hindi_cinema "Hindi cinema") of India. Mumbai is often compared to [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"),[[31]](#cite_note-MumbaiNYCTwins-32)[[32]](#cite_note-MumbaiNewYorkOfIndia-33) and is home to the [Bombay Stock Exchange](/wiki/Bombay_Stock_Exchange "Bombay Stock Exchange"), situated on [Dalal Street](/wiki/Dalal_Street "Dalal Street"). It is also one of the world's top ten centres of commerce in terms of global financial flow,[[33]](#cite_note-34) generating 6.16% of India's [GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product"),[[34]](#cite_note-mmrda_muip_gdp-35) and accounting for 25% of the nation's industrial output, 70% of [maritime](/wiki/Maritime_transport "Maritime transport") trade in India (via [Mumbai Port](/wiki/Mumbai_Port "Mumbai Port"), [Jawaharlal Nehru Port](/wiki/Jawaharlal_Nehru_Port "Jawaharlal Nehru Port") and [Dharamtar Port](/wiki/Dharamtar_Port "Dharamtar Port")),[[35]](#cite_note-36) and 70% of capital transactions to [India's economy](/wiki/Economy_of_India "Economy of India").[[36]](#cite_note-NMIAtender-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38) The city houses important financial institutions and the corporate [headquarters](/wiki/List_of_Indian_companies_headquartered_in_Mumbai "List of Indian companies headquartered in Mumbai") of numerous [Indian companies](/wiki/List_of_Indian_companies "List of Indian companies") and [multinational corporations](/wiki/Multinational_corporations "Multinational corporations"). The city is also home to some of India's premier scientific and nuclear institutes and the [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi_cinema "Hindi cinema") and [Marathi](/wiki/Marathi_cinema "Marathi cinema") film industries. Mumbai is also the home of [Bollywood](/wiki/Bollywood "Bollywood") (Bombay Cinema), which is considered to be the second largest [film industry](/wiki/Film_industry "Film industry") after [Hollywood.](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood, Los Angeles")[[38]](#cite_note-39) Mumbai's business opportunities attract migrants from all over India.

## Etymology

The name *Mumbai* ([Marathi](/wiki/Marathi_language "Marathi language"): मुंबई) originated from *Mumbā* or *Mahā-Ambā*—the name of the patron Hindu goddess ([Kula Devata](/wiki/Kuladevata "Kuladevata")) [Mumbadevi](/wiki/Mumbadevi "Mumbadevi") of the native [Koli](/wiki/Koli_people "Koli people") community[[39]](#cite_note-40)—and from *ā'ī*, meaning "mother" in [Marathi](/wiki/Marathi_language "Marathi language").[[23]](#cite_note-james_customs-24)[[40]](#cite_note-41) By some accounts, the Koli community of [Kathiawar](/wiki/Kathiawar "Kathiawar") and [Central Gujarat](/wiki/Central_Gujarat "Central Gujarat") introduced their deity Mumba from Kathiawar, where her worship continues to this day.[[24]](#cite_note-munshi-25)[[25]](#cite_note-rnmehta-26) However, other sources disagree.[[25]](#cite_note-rnmehta-26)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Mumbadevi_temple.jpg/250px-Mumbadevi_temple.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mumbadevi_temple.jpg)

The [Mumba Devi Temple](/wiki/Mumba_Devi_Temple "Mumba Devi Temple"), from whom the city of Mumbai may derive its name.

The oldest known names for the city are *Kakamuchee* and *Galajunkja*; these are sometimes still used.[[41]](#cite_note-42)[[42]](#cite_note-43) Portuguese writer [Gaspar Correia](/wiki/Gaspar_Correia "Gaspar Correia") recorded the name "Bombaim" after 1512 in his *Lendas da Índia* (*Legends of India*).[[43]](#cite_note-44)[[44]](#cite_note-et6yh-45) Some [Anglophone](/wiki/English-speaking_world "English-speaking world") authors have suggested this came from a supposed [Galician–Portuguese](/wiki/Galician-Portuguese "Galician-Portuguese") phrase *bom baim*, "good little bay",[[45]](#cite_note-Shirodkar_1998_7-46) with no scientific basis.[[46]](#cite_note-jpm2-47)[[b]](#cite_note-48) In 1516, Portuguese explorer [Duarte Barbosa](/wiki/Duarte_Barbosa "Duarte Barbosa") used the name *Tana-Maiambu*: *Tana* appears to refer to the adjoining town of [Thane](/wiki/Thane "Thane") and *Maiambu* to *Mumbadevi*.[[47]](#cite_note-49) The form *Bombaim* is still commonly used in Portuguese.[[48]](#cite_note-ety-50) Many variant names were recorded in the 16th and the 17th centuries.[[c]](#cite_note-53) After the [English](/wiki/English_overseas_possessions "English overseas possessions") gained possession of the city in the 17th century, the Portuguese name was [anglicised](/wiki/Anglicise "Anglicise") to *Bombay*.[[51]](#cite_note-54) Ali Muhammad Khan, imperial [dewan](/wiki/Dewan "Dewan") or revenue minister of the Gujarat province, in the *Mirat-i Ahmedi* (1762) called the city *Manbai*.[[52]](#cite_note-55)

The French traveller [Louis Rousselet](/wiki/Louis_Rousselet "Louis Rousselet"), who visited in 1863 and 1868, stated in 1877 that "Etymologists have wrongly derived this name from the Portuguese Bôa Bahia, or (French: "bonne baie", English: "good bay"), not knowing that the tutelar goddess of this island has been, from remote antiquity, Bomba, or [Mumba Devi](/wiki/Mumba_Devi_Temple "Mumba Devi Temple"), and that she still ... possesses a temple".[[53]](#cite_note-56) British officer and scholar [John Briggs](/wiki/John_Briggs_(East_India_Company_officer) "John Briggs (East India Company officer)") concurred that the name Bombay was a corruption of "Mumby", for a temple to Mumba Devi.[[54]](#cite_note-57)

By the late 20th century, the city was called *Mumbai* or *Mambai* in Marathi, [Konkani](/wiki/Konkani_language "Konkani language"), [Gujarati](/wiki/Gujarati_language "Gujarati language"), [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada_language "Kannada language") and [Sindhi](/wiki/Sindhi_language "Sindhi language"), and *Bambai* in [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi").[[55]](#cite_note-Christopher_Beam-58) The Government of India officially changed the English name to *Mumbai* in November 1995.[[56]](#cite_note-59) This came at the insistence of the Marathi nationalist [Shiv Sena](/wiki/Shiv_Sena_(1966%E2%80%932022) "Shiv Sena (1966–2022)") party, which had just won the Maharashtra state elections, and mirrored [similar name changes across India](/wiki/Renaming_of_cities_in_India "Renaming of cities in India").[[57]](#cite_note-60) Shiv Sena argued that the name 'Bombay' echoed British colonial rule.[[58]](#cite_note-61)[[59]](#cite_note-62) While Mumbai is still called Bombay by some residents and by some Indians from other regions,[[60]](#cite_note-24change-63)[[61]](#cite_note-mummaharashtra1-64) mention of the city by a name other than *Mumbai* has become controversial.[[62]](#cite_note-65)[[63]](#cite_note-66)

### People from Mumbai

A resident of Mumbai is called *Mumbaikar* (pronounced [[ˈmumbəikəɾ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Marathi "Help:IPA/Marathi")) in [Marathi](/wiki/Marathi_language "Marathi language"), in which the suffix *-kar* means a 'resident of'. The term had been in use for quite some time, but it gained popularity after the official name change to Mumbai.[[64]](#cite_note-Mumbaikar-67) Older terms such as *Bombayite* are used infrequently.[[65]](#cite_note-68)[[66]](#cite_note-69)

### Early history

Mumbai is built on what was once an archipelago of [seven islands](/wiki/Seven_islands_of_Bombay "Seven islands of Bombay"): [Isle of Bombay](/wiki/Isle_of_Bombay "Isle of Bombay"), [Parel](/wiki/Parel "Parel"), [Mazagaon](/wiki/Mazagaon "Mazagaon"), [Mahim](/wiki/Mahim "Mahim"), [Colaba](/wiki/Colaba "Colaba"), [Worli](/wiki/Worli "Worli"), and [Old Woman's Island](/wiki/Old_Woman%27s_Island "Old Woman's Island") (also known as *Little Colaba*).[[67]](#cite_note-70) [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene") sediments found along the coast near [Kandivali](/wiki/Kandivali "Kandivali") suggest that the islands were inhabited since the [South Asian Stone Age](/wiki/South_Asian_Stone_Age "South Asian Stone Age").[[68]](#cite_note-71) In the 3rd century BCE, the islands formed part of the [Maurya Empire](/wiki/Maurya_Empire "Maurya Empire"), ruled by the emperor [Ashoka](/wiki/Ashoka "Ashoka").[[69]](#cite_note-72) The islands were perhaps occupied by the [Koli](/wiki/Koli_people "Koli people") fishing community during the beginning of the [Common Era](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era").[[70]](#cite_note-73)[[71]](#cite_note-city-profile-74)

[![A white Buddhist stupa.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Mumbai_03-2016_94_Kanheri_Caves.jpg/250px-Mumbai_03-2016_94_Kanheri_Caves.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mumbai_03-2016_94_Kanheri_Caves.jpg)

The [Kanheri Caves](/wiki/Kanheri_Caves "Kanheri Caves") contain Buddhist artworks from the 1st to the 10th century CE.

The [Kanheri Caves](/wiki/Kanheri_Caves "Kanheri Caves") in [Borivali](/wiki/Borivali "Borivali") were excavated in the first century CE,[[72]](#cite_note-Ray-75) and served as a centre of [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") in Western India.[[73]](#cite_note-76) The [Mahakali Caves](/wiki/Mahakali_Caves "Mahakali Caves") in [Andheri](/wiki/Andheri "Andheri") were cut out between the 1st century BCE and the 6th century CE.[[74]](#cite_note-battleneglect-77)[[75]](#cite_note-78) Greek geographer [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") referred to the islands as *Heptanesia* ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek"): A cluster of seven islands) in 150 CE.[[76]](#cite_note-79) Between the 2nd century BCE and 9th century CE, the islands came under the control of successive [indigenous dynasties](/wiki/History_of_Mumbai_under_indigenous_empires "History of Mumbai under indigenous empires")–[Satavahanas](/wiki/Satavahana_dynasty "Satavahana dynasty"), [Western Satraps](/wiki/Western_Satraps "Western Satraps"), [Abhira](/wiki/Abhira_kingdom "Abhira kingdom"), [Vakataka](/wiki/Vakataka_dynasty "Vakataka dynasty"), [Kalachuris](/wiki/Kalachuris_of_Mahishmati "Kalachuris of Mahishmati"), [Konkan Mauryas](/wiki/Mauryas_of_Puri "Mauryas of Puri"), [Chalukyas](/wiki/Chalukya_dynasty "Chalukya dynasty"), and [Rashtrakutas](/wiki/Rashtrakuta_dynasty "Rashtrakuta dynasty"),[[77]](#cite_note-80) before being ruled by the [Shilaharas](/wiki/Shilahara "Shilahara") from 810 to 1260 CE.[[78]](#cite_note-81) Some of the oldest edifices in the city are the [Jogeshwari Caves](/wiki/Jogeshwari_Caves "Jogeshwari Caves") (between 520 and 525 CE),[[79]](#cite_note-82) [Elephanta Caves](/wiki/Elephanta_Caves "Elephanta Caves") (between 6th to 7th century CE),[[80]](#cite_note-83) [Walkeshwar Temple](/wiki/Walkeshwar_Temple "Walkeshwar Temple") (10th century CE),[[81]](#cite_note-84)[[82]](#cite_note-85) and [Banganga Tank](/wiki/Banganga_Tank "Banganga Tank") (12th century CE).[[83]](#cite_note-86)[[84]](#cite_note-87)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Hajiali.jpg/250px-Hajiali.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hajiali.jpg)

The [Haji Ali Dargah](/wiki/Haji_Ali_Dargah "Haji Ali Dargah") was built in 1431, when Mumbai was under the rule of the [Gujarat Sultanate](/wiki/Gujarat_Sultanate "Gujarat Sultanate").

King [Bhima](/wiki/Bhima_of_Mahikavati "Bhima of Mahikavati") founded his kingdom in the region in the late 13th century CE and established his capital in Mahikawati (present day [Mahim](/wiki/Mahim "Mahim")).[[85]](#cite_note-88) The [Pathare Prabhus](/wiki/Pathare_Prabhu "Pathare Prabhu"), among the earliest known settlers of the city, came to Mahikawati from [Saurashtra](/wiki/Saurashtra_(region) "Saurashtra (region)") in Gujarat around 1298 CE.[[86]](#cite_note-89) The [Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate") annexed the islands in 1347–48 CE and controlled it until 1407 CE. During this time, the islands were administered by the [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") governors of Gujarat, appointed by the sultanate.[[87]](#cite_note-90)[[88]](#cite_note-91) The islands were later governed by the independent [Gujarat Sultanate](/wiki/Gujarat_Sultanate "Gujarat Sultanate"), established in 1407CE. Numerous mosques were built during the period, including the [Haji Ali Dargah](/wiki/Haji_Ali_Dargah "Haji Ali Dargah") in [Worli](/wiki/Worli "Worli"), erected in 1431, as a homage to [Sufi saint](/wiki/Sufi_saint "Sufi saint") Haji Ali.[[89]](#cite_note-92) From 1429 to 1431 CE, the islands were a source of contention between the Gujarat Sultanate and the [Bahmani Sultanate](/wiki/Bahmani_Sultanate "Bahmani Sultanate") of the [Deccan](/wiki/Deccan "Deccan").[[90]](#cite_note-93)[[91]](#cite_note-mis-94) In 1493 CE, [Bahadur Khan Gilani](/wiki/Bahadur_Khan_Gilani "Bahadur Khan Gilani") of the Bahmani Sultanate attempted to conquer the islands but was repulsed.[[92]](#cite_note-95)

### Portuguese and British rule

Main articles: [History of Bombay under Portuguese rule (1534–1661)](/wiki/History_of_Bombay_under_Portuguese_rule_(1534%E2%80%931661) "History of Bombay under Portuguese rule (1534–1661)") and [History of Bombay under British rule](/wiki/History_of_Bombay_under_British_rule "History of Bombay under British rule")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Madh-fort3.jpg/250px-Madh-fort3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Madh-fort3.jpg)

[Madh Fort](/wiki/Madh_Fort "Madh Fort"), built by the [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_India "Portuguese India")

Apprehensive of the power of the [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") under emperor [Humayun](/wiki/Humayun "Humayun"), sultan [Bahadur Shah of Gujarat](/wiki/Bahadur_Shah_of_Gujarat "Bahadur Shah of Gujarat") signed the [Treaty of Bassein](/wiki/Treaty_of_Bassein_(1534) "Treaty of Bassein (1534)") with the [Portuguese Empire](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire") on 23 December 1534. As per the treaty, the seven islands of Bombay, the nearby town of [Bassein](/wiki/Vasai "Vasai"), and its dependencies were given to the Portuguese on 25 October 1535.[[93]](#cite_note-96)

The Portuguese leased the islands to various Portuguese officers during their regime, and assisted the [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") religious orders.[[94]](#cite_note-97) The islands were called by various names, and later took the written form *Bombaim*. The Portuguese [Franciscans](/wiki/Franciscans "Franciscans") and [Jesuits](/wiki/Jesuits "Jesuits") built churches in the city, such as the [St. Michael's Church](/wiki/St._Michael%27s_Church_(Mumbai) "St. Michael's Church (Mumbai)") at [Mahim](/wiki/Mahim "Mahim") (1534),[[95]](#cite_note-98) [St. John the Baptist Church](/wiki/St._John_the_Baptist_Church,_Mumbai "St. John the Baptist Church, Mumbai") at [Andheri](/wiki/Andheri "Andheri") (1579),[[96]](#cite_note-99) [St. Andrew's Church](/wiki/St._Andrew%27s_Church_(Mumbai) "St. Andrew's Church (Mumbai)") at [Bandra](/wiki/Bandra "Bandra") (1580),[[97]](#cite_note-100) and [Gloria Church](/wiki/Gloria_Church "Gloria Church") at [Byculla](/wiki/Byculla "Byculla") (1632).[[98]](#cite_note-101) They also built several fortifications including the [Bombay Castle](/wiki/Bombay_Castle "Bombay Castle"), [Castella de Aguada](/wiki/Castella_de_Aguada "Castella de Aguada"), and [Madh Fort](/wiki/Madh_Fort "Madh Fort").[[99]](#cite_note-102) The [English](/wiki/English_Empire "English Empire") vied with the Portuguese for hegemony over Mumbai, recognising its strategic natural harbour and its separation from the main land.[[100]](#cite_note-103) On 11 May 1661 CE, the marriage treaty of [Charles II of England](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") and [Catherine of Braganza](/wiki/Catherine_of_Braganza "Catherine of Braganza"), daughter of [King John IV of Portugal](/wiki/John_IV_of_Portugal "John IV of Portugal"), gave most of the islands to the English, as part of Catherine's [dowry](/wiki/Dowry "Dowry") to Charles.[[101]](#cite_note-104)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/AMH-6748-NA_Two_views_of_the_English_fort_in_Bombay.jpg/250px-AMH-6748-NA_Two_views_of_the_English_fort_in_Bombay.jpg)](/wiki/File:AMH-6748-NA_Two_views_of_the_English_fort_in_Bombay.jpg)

Views of the English fort in Bombay, c. 1665

In accordance with the [Royal Charter of 27 March 1668](/wiki/Royal_Charter_of_27_March_1668 "Royal Charter of 27 March 1668"), England leased these islands to the [English East India Company](/wiki/English_East_India_Company "English East India Company") in 1668 for a sum of [£](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling")10 per annum.[[102]](#cite_note-105) The population quickly rose from 10,000 in 1661 to 60,000 in 1675.[[103]](#cite_note-106) The islands were subject to several attacks such as by [Yakut Khan](/wiki/Yakut_Khan "Yakut Khan"), the Muslim Koli admiral of the Mughal Empire, in October 1672,[[104]](#cite_note-107)[[105]](#cite_note-108)[[106]](#cite_note-109) [Rickloffe van Goen](/wiki/Rickloffe_van_Goen "Rickloffe van Goen"), the governor-general of [Dutch India](/wiki/Dutch_East_India_Company "Dutch East India Company") on 20 February 1673,[[107]](#cite_note-110) and [Siddi](/wiki/Siddi "Siddi") admiral [Sambal](/wiki/Siddi_Sambal "Siddi Sambal") on 10 October 1673.[[108]](#cite_note-111) In 1687, the English East India Company transferred its headquarters from [Surat](/wiki/Surat "Surat") to Mumbai, and the city eventually became the headquarters of the [Bombay Presidency](/wiki/Bombay_Presidency "Bombay Presidency").[[109]](#cite_note-112)[[110]](#cite_note-113) The islands again suffered incursions from [Yakut Khan](/wiki/Yakut_Khan "Yakut Khan") in 1689–90.[[111]](#cite_note-114)

The Portuguese presence in the region ended when the [Marathas](/wiki/Maratha_Confederacy "Maratha Confederacy") under [Peshwa](/wiki/Peshwa "Peshwa") [Baji Rao I](/wiki/Baji_Rao_I "Baji Rao I") captured [Salsette](/wiki/Salsette_Island "Salsette Island") in 1737, and Bassein in 1739.[[112]](#cite_note-115) By the middle of the 18th century, Bombay expanded into a major trading town, receiving a large influx of migrants from across India.[[113]](#cite_note-116) The British occupied Salsette on 28 December 1774, and signed the [Treaty of Surat](/wiki/Treaty_of_Surat "Treaty of Surat") (1775), with Raghunathrao, one of the claimants to the Maratha throne, to transfer control of [Salsette](/wiki/Salsette "Salsette") and Bassein.[[114]](#cite_note-117) This resulted in the [First Anglo-Maratha War](/wiki/First_Anglo-Maratha_War "First Anglo-Maratha War"), and the British secured Salsette officially through the Treaty of Purandar (1776),[[115]](#cite_note-118) and later through the [Treaty of Salbai](/wiki/Treaty_of_Salbai "Treaty of Salbai") (1782), signed to settle the outcome of the war.[[116]](#cite_note-119)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Ships_in_Bombay_Harbour%2C_1731.jpg/250px-Ships_in_Bombay_Harbour%2C_1731.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ships_in_Bombay_Harbour,_1731.jpg)

Ships at [Bombay Harbour](/wiki/Bombay_Harbour "Bombay Harbour") (c. 1731)

From 1782 onwards, the English undertook large-scale civil engineering projects to merge the seven islands of Bombay into one by means of a [causeway](/wiki/Causeway "Causeway"), the [Hornby Vellard](/wiki/Hornby_Vellard "Hornby Vellard"), completed by 1784.[[27]](#cite_note-Dwivedi_2001_28-28)[[117]](#cite_note-120) In 1817, the English East India Company under [Mountstuart Elphinstone](/wiki/Mountstuart_Elphinstone "Mountstuart Elphinstone") defeated [Baji Rao II](/wiki/Baji_Rao_II "Baji Rao II"), the last Peshwa of the Marathas in the [Battle of Khadki](/wiki/Battle_of_Khadki "Battle of Khadki").[[118]](#cite_note-121) The success of the campaign marked the end of attacks by native powers, and brought almost the whole of the [Deccan Plateau](/wiki/Deccan_Plateau "Deccan Plateau") under the British suzerainty and the Bombay Presidency.[[119]](#cite_note-122) On 16 April 1853, India's first passenger railway line was established, connecting Bombay to the neighbouring town of [Thana](/wiki/Thane "Thane").[[120]](#cite_note-123) During the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") (1861–1865), the city became the world's chief [cotton](/wiki/Cotton "Cotton")-trading market, resulting in an economic boom.[[121]](#cite_note-124)

The opening of the [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez_Canal "Suez Canal") in 1869 transformed Bombay into one of the largest seaports on the [Arabian Sea](/wiki/Arabian_Sea "Arabian Sea").[[122]](#cite_note-125) In September 1896, the city was [hit](/wiki/Mumbai_plague_epidemic "Mumbai plague epidemic") by a [bubonic plague](/wiki/Bubonic_plague "Bubonic plague") epidemic, which resulted in an over 20,000 people,[[123]](#cite_note-126) and a large proportion of the population fleeing the city, harming the textile industry.[[124]](#cite_note-127) The [Indian independence movement](/wiki/Indian_independence_movement "Indian independence movement") fostered the [Quit India Movement](/wiki/Quit_India_Movement "Quit India Movement") in 1942 at the Bombay session of the [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress") and the [Royal Indian Navy mutiny](/wiki/Royal_Indian_Navy_mutiny "Royal Indian Navy mutiny") in 1946.[[125]](#cite_note-128)[[126]](#cite_note-129)

### Independent India

Main article: [History of Bombay in Independent India](/wiki/History_of_Bombay_in_Independent_India "History of Bombay in Independent India")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Victoria_Terminus%2C_Bombay_in_1950.jpg/250px-Victoria_Terminus%2C_Bombay_in_1950.jpg)](/wiki/File:Victoria_Terminus,_Bombay_in_1950.jpg)

[Municipal Corporation Building](/wiki/Municipal_Corporation_Building,_Mumbai "Municipal Corporation Building, Mumbai") (center) with the [Victoria Terminus](/wiki/Victoria_Terminus "Victoria Terminus") (left) in 1950

After India's independence in 1947, the Bombay Presidency was restructured into [Bombay State](/wiki/Bombay_State "Bombay State"), which was further expanded when several erstwhile [princely states](/wiki/Princely_states "Princely states") were integrated into the Indian union. Subsequently, the city became the capital of Bombay State.[[127]](#cite_note-130) In April 1950, municipal limits were expanded by merging the [Mumbai suburban](/wiki/Mumbai_suburban_district "Mumbai suburban district") and [city districts](/wiki/Mumbai_city_district "Mumbai city district") to form the Greater Mumbai Municipal Corporation.[[128]](#cite_note-131)

The [Samyukta Maharashtra movement](/wiki/Samyukta_Maharashtra_movement "Samyukta Maharashtra movement") to establish a separate [Maharashtra](/wiki/Maharashtra "Maharashtra") state reached its heights in the 1950s. In 1955, during discussions in the [Parliament of India](/wiki/Parliament_of_India "Parliament of India"), the Congress party demanded that the city be made an autonomous city-state.[[129]](#cite_note-132) While the [States Reorganisation Committee](/wiki/States_Reorganisation_Act "States Reorganisation Act") recommended a bilingual state for Maharashtra–[Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat") with Bombay as its capital in its 1955 report, the [Bombay Citizens' Committee](/wiki/Bombay_Citizens%27_Committee "Bombay Citizens' Committee"), an advocacy group of [Gujarati](/wiki/Gujarati_people "Gujarati people") industrialists, lobbied for an independent status for the city.[[130]](#cite_note-133) Following several protests in which 105 people died in clashes with the law enforcement agencies, Bombay State was reorganised on linguistic lines into separate states of Maharashtra and Gujarat on 1 May 1960.[[131]](#cite_note-134) The Gujarati-speaking areas were separated from Bombay State to form Gujarat.[[132]](#cite_note-135) Maharashtra was established with Bombay as its capital was formed with the merger of [Marathi](/wiki/Marathi_language "Marathi language")-speaking areas of Bombay State, eight districts from [Central Provinces and Berar](/wiki/Central_Provinces_and_Berar "Central Provinces and Berar"), five districts from [Hyderabad State](/wiki/Hyderabad_State "Hyderabad State"), and numerous princely states.[[133]](#cite_note-136) As a memorial to the martyrs of the Samyukta Maharashtra movement, [Flora Fountain](/wiki/Flora_Fountain "Flora Fountain") was renamed as *[Hutatma Chowk](/wiki/Hutatma_Chowk "Hutatma Chowk")* (Martyr's Square) and a memorial was erected.[[134]](#cite_note-137)

[![A stone statue of torch-bearers as seen at night. A fountain with a white base is in the background](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Hutatma_Chowk.jpg/250px-Hutatma_Chowk.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hutatma_Chowk.jpg)

The [Hutatma Chowk](/wiki/Hutatma_Chowk "Hutatma Chowk") memorial honours the martyrs of the [Samyukta Maharashtra Samiti](/wiki/Samyukta_Maharashtra_Samiti "Samyukta Maharashtra Samiti")

The following decades saw a massive expansion of the city and its suburbs. In the late 1960s, [Nariman Point](/wiki/Nariman_Point "Nariman Point") and [Cuffe Parade](/wiki/Cuffe_Parade "Cuffe Parade") were reclaimed from the sea and developed.[[135]](#cite_note-138) Industrial development began with a focus on the petrochemical, electronic, and automotive sectors. In the 1950s, [Hindustan Petroleum](/wiki/Hindustan_Petroleum "Hindustan Petroleum") and [Bharat Petroleum](/wiki/Bharat_Petroleum "Bharat Petroleum") commissioned [Mumbai Refinery](/wiki/Mumbai_Refinery_(HPCL) "Mumbai Refinery (HPCL)") at [Trombay](/wiki/Trombay "Trombay") and [BPCL Refinery](/wiki/Mumbai_Refinery_(BPCL) "Mumbai Refinery (BPCL)") respectively.[[136]](#cite_note-139) The [Bombay Metropolitan Region Development Authority](/wiki/Mumbai_Metropolitan_Region_Development_Authority "Mumbai Metropolitan Region Development Authority") was established on 26 January 1975 by the [Government of Maharashtra](/wiki/Government_of_Maharashtra "Government of Maharashtra") to co-ordinate development in the [Mumbai metropolitan area](/wiki/Mumbai_metropolitan_area "Mumbai metropolitan area").[[137]](#cite_note-140) In August 1979, a sister township of [New Bombay](/wiki/New_Bombay "New Bombay") was established by the [City and Industrial Development Corporation](/wiki/City_and_Industrial_Development_Corporation "City and Industrial Development Corporation") spread across the [Thane](/wiki/Thane_district "Thane district") and [Raigad districts](/wiki/Raigad_district "Raigad district") to accommodate the city's expanding population. The [Great Bombay Textile Strike](/wiki/Great_Bombay_Textile_Strike "Great Bombay Textile Strike") in 1982, which involved nearly 250,000 workers in more than 50 textile mills, resulted in the downfall of the sector.[[138]](#cite_note-141) The defunct cotton mills of the city have since become the focus of [redevelopment](/wiki/Redevelopment_of_Mumbai_mills "Redevelopment of Mumbai mills") in the 21st century.[[139]](#cite_note-142)[[140]](#cite_note-143)

The [Jawaharlal Nehru Port](/wiki/Jawaharlal_Nehru_Port "Jawaharlal Nehru Port") was commissioned on 26 May 1989 across the creek at [Nhava Sheva](/wiki/Nhava_Sheva "Nhava Sheva") to de-congest the [Mumbai Harbour](/wiki/Mumbai_Harbour "Mumbai Harbour") and to serve as a hub port for the city.[[141]](#cite_note-144) The geographical limit of Greater Bombay, was coextensive with its municipal limit. On 1 October 1990, it was bifurcated to form the city and suburban revenue districts, though they continued to be administered by the same municipal administration.[[142]](#cite_note-145)

The 1990s and 2000s saw a slew of violence and terrorism incidents. Following the [demolition of the Babri Masjid](/wiki/Demolition_of_the_Babri_Masjid "Demolition of the Babri Masjid") in [Ayodhya](/wiki/Ayodhya "Ayodhya"), the city was rocked by the [Hindu-Muslim riots of 1992–93](/wiki/Bombay_riots "Bombay riots") in which more than 1,000 people were killed.[[143]](#cite_note-146) In March 1993, [a series of 13 coordinated bombings](/wiki/1993_Bombay_bombings "1993 Bombay bombings") at city landmarks by [Islamic extremists](/wiki/Islamic_extremism "Islamic extremism") and the [Mumbai underworld](/wiki/Mumbai_underworld#Mumbai_underworld "Mumbai underworld") resulted in 257 deaths and over 700 injuries.[[144]](#cite_note-147) In 2006, 209 people were killed and over 700 injured when [seven bombs exploded](/wiki/2006_Mumbai_train_bombings "2006 Mumbai train bombings") on the city's [commuter trains](/wiki/Mumbai_Suburban_Railway "Mumbai Suburban Railway").[[145]](#cite_note-148) In 2008, a series of [ten coordinated attacks](/wiki/2008_Mumbai_attacks "2008 Mumbai attacks") by armed terrorists for three days resulted in 173 deaths, 308 injuries, and severe damage to several heritage landmarks and prestigious hotels.[[146]](#cite_note-149) The three coordinated [bomb explosions in July 2011](/wiki/2011_Mumbai_bombings "2011 Mumbai bombings") that occurred at the [Opera house](/wiki/Opera_house "Opera house"), [Zaveri Bazaar](/wiki/Zaveri_Bazaar "Zaveri Bazaar") and [Dadar](/wiki/Dadar "Dadar") were the latest in the series of terrorist attacks in Mumbai which resulted in 26 deaths and 130 injuries.[[147]](#cite_note-150)[[148]](#cite_note-151)

In the 21st century, Mumbai has become the commercial capital of India and a global financial hub.[[149]](#cite_note-Mumbai_global-152) It is the home of India's main financial services companies and a focus for infrastructure development and private investment.[[150]](#cite_note-153) It has become one of South Asia's largest cities and home of the world's most prolific [film industry](/wiki/Bollywood "Bollywood").[[151]](#cite_note-154)

## Geography

Main articles: [Geography of Mumbai](/wiki/Geography_of_Mumbai "Geography of Mumbai") and [Geology of Mumbai](/wiki/Geology_of_Mumbai "Geology of Mumbai")

See also: [Eastern Suburbs (Mumbai)](/wiki/Eastern_Suburbs_(Mumbai) "Eastern Suburbs (Mumbai)"), [Western Suburbs (Mumbai)](/wiki/Western_Suburbs_(Mumbai) "Western Suburbs (Mumbai)"), and [List of neighbourhoods in Mumbai](/wiki/List_of_neighbourhoods_in_Mumbai "List of neighbourhoods in Mumbai")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/57/Mumbaicitydistricts.png)](/wiki/File:Mumbaicitydistricts.png)

Mumbai consists of two districts, [Mumbai City](/wiki/Mumbai_City_district "Mumbai City district") and [Mumbai Suburban](/wiki/Mumbai_Suburban_district "Mumbai Suburban district")

Mumbai is spread over an area of 603 km2 (233 sq mi),[[152]](#cite_note-155) and is divided into two districts–[Mumbai City](/wiki/Mumbai_City_district "Mumbai City district") and [Mumbai Suburban](/wiki/Mumbai_Suburban_district "Mumbai Suburban district").[[153]](#cite_note-156) The suburban district occupies 446 km2 (172 sq mi) and is divided into three [tehsils](/wiki/Tehsils "Tehsils")–[Andheri](/wiki/Andheri "Andheri"), [Borivali](/wiki/Borivali "Borivali"), and [Kurla](/wiki/Kurla "Kurla").[[154]](#cite_note-157) The city district-region located to the south is referred to as [South Mumbai](/wiki/South_Mumbai "South Mumbai") or "Island City", and occupies an area of 157 km2 (61 sq mi).[[34]](#cite_note-mmrda_muip_gdp-35)[[155]](#cite_note-158) The [Mumbai Metropolitan Region](/wiki/Mumbai_Metropolitan_Region "Mumbai Metropolitan Region") includes portions of [Thane](/wiki/Thane_district "Thane district"), [Palghar](/wiki/Palghar_district "Palghar district") and [Raigad districts](/wiki/Raigad_district "Raigad district") in addition to Greater Mumbai, and covers an area of 4,355 km2 (1,681 sq mi).[[7]](#cite_note-projectsecoa1-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Mumbai_432_pan_crop_375_%2827105063465%29.jpg/250px-Mumbai_432_pan_crop_375_%2827105063465%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mumbai_432_pan_crop_375_(27105063465).jpg)

[Satellite image](/wiki/Satellite_image "Satellite image") of Mumbai

Mumbai is on a narrow [peninsula](/wiki/Peninsula "Peninsula") located on the [Salsette Island](/wiki/Salsette_Island "Salsette Island"), which is bound by the [Arabian Sea](/wiki/Arabian_Sea "Arabian Sea") to the west, [Thane Creek](/wiki/Thane_Creek "Thane Creek") to the east, and [Vasai Creek](/wiki/Vasai_Creek "Vasai Creek") to the north.[[156]](#cite_note-159)[[157]](#cite_note-mloc-160) It lies at the mouth of the [Ulhas River](/wiki/Ulhas_River "Ulhas River") on the [western coast](/wiki/Western_Coastal_Plains "Western Coastal Plains") of India, in the [Konkan](/wiki/Konkan "Konkan") region.[[158]](#cite_note-geo-161) It shares the Salsette Island, with the Thane district. [Navi Mumbai](/wiki/Navi_Mumbai "Navi Mumbai") is located to the east of the Thane Creek, and [Thane](/wiki/Thane "Thane") is situated towards the north of the Vasai Creek.[[159]](#cite_note-162) Majority of the city lies just above the [mean sea level](/wiki/Mean_sea_level "Mean sea level"), with elevations ranging from 10 to 15 m (33 to 49 ft),[[160]](#cite_note-163) and an average elevation of 14 m (46 ft).[[161]](#cite_note-Wbase-164) Northern Mumbai consists of hilly terrain,[[162]](#cite_note-165) with the highest point in the city at 450 m (1,480 ft) in the [Powai](/wiki/Powai "Powai")–[Kanheri](/wiki/Kanheri "Kanheri") range.[[163]](#cite_note-166) The coastline is indented with [creeks](/wiki/Creek_(tidal) "Creek (tidal)") and bays, stretching from the Thane creek in the east to Madh Marve on the western front.[[164]](#cite_note-167) The eastern coast of the Salsette Island is covered with [mangrove](/wiki/Mangrove "Mangrove") [swamps](/wiki/Swamp "Swamp"), while the western coast is mostly sandy and rocky.[[165]](#cite_note-168)

Soil cover in the city region is predominantly sandy. In the suburbs, the soil cover is largely [alluvial](/wiki/Alluvial_soil "Alluvial soil") and [loamy](/wiki/Loamy_soil "Loamy soil").[[166]](#cite_note-169) The underlying rock is black [Deccan](/wiki/Deccan_Plateau "Deccan Plateau") basalt, dating to the late [Cretaceous](/wiki/Cretaceous "Cretaceous") and early [Eocene](/wiki/Eocene "Eocene") eras.[[167]](#cite_note-170) Mumbai sits on a [seismically active](/wiki/Seismicity "Seismicity") zone with 23 [fault lines](/wiki/Fault_line "Fault line").[[168]](#cite_note-171) The area is a [Seismic Zone III region](/wiki/Earthquake_hazard_zoning_of_India "Earthquake hazard zoning of India"),[[169]](#cite_note-172) which means an earthquake of up to magnitude 6.5 on the [Richter magnitude scale](/wiki/Richter_magnitude_scale "Richter magnitude scale") could occur.[[170]](#cite_note-173)

The [Sanjay Gandhi National Park](/wiki/Sanjay_Gandhi_National_Park "Sanjay Gandhi National Park") (Borivali National Park) is situated partly in the Mumbai suburban and Thane districts, and covers an area of 103.09 km2 (39.80 sq mi).[[171]](#cite_note-174) There are two major lakes in the city– [Tulsi Lake](/wiki/Tulsi_Lake "Tulsi Lake") and [Vihar Lake](/wiki/Vihar_Lake "Vihar Lake"). The city also draws its water supply from various dams located in Thane district including [Bhatsa](/wiki/Bhatsa_Dam "Bhatsa Dam"), [Lower Vaitarna](/wiki/Lower_Vaitarna_Dam "Lower Vaitarna Dam"), [Middle Vaitarna](/wiki/Middle_Vaitarna_Dam "Middle Vaitarna Dam"), [Tansa](/wiki/Tansa_Dam "Tansa Dam"), and [Upper Vaitarna](/wiki/Upper_Vaitarna_Dam "Upper Vaitarna Dam").[[172]](#cite_note-175) The [Powai Lake](/wiki/Powai_Lake "Powai Lake") is an artificial lake within the city limits, is used only for agricultural and industrial purposes.[[173]](#cite_note-176) The [Mithi River](/wiki/Mithi_River "Mithi River") is an urban river formed by the [tailwater](/wiki/Tailwater "Tailwater") discharges of the Powai and Vihar lakes, and is highly polluted.[[174]](#cite_note-177)

### Climate

Main article: [Climate of Mumbai](/wiki/Climate_of_Mumbai "Climate of Mumbai")

Mumbai has a [tropical wet and dry climate](/wiki/Tropical_savanna_climate "Tropical savanna climate"), classified as *Aw* under the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"). However, the central and northern suburbs experience a [tropical monsoon climate](/wiki/Tropical_monsoon_climate "Tropical monsoon climate") (*Am*) with heavier rainfall during the wet season. Mumbai has a nearly rainless stretch from October to May, with its most intense rainfall during July.[[176]](#cite_note-179) A cooler season from December to February is followed by a hotter season from March to May. The period from June to the end of September constitutes the [southwest monsoon](/wiki/Monsoon_of_Indian_subcontinent "Monsoon of Indian subcontinent") season.[[177]](#cite_note-180)

The average annual temperature is 27 °C (81 °F).[[178]](#cite_note-Rohli_2007_267-181) In the city, the average maximum and minimum temperatures are 31 °C (88 °F) and 24 °C (75 °F) respetively. In the suburbs, the daily mean maximum temperature ranges from 29 °C (84 °F) to 33 °C (91 °F), and the daily mean minimum temperature ranges from 16 °C (61 °F) to 26 °C (79 °F).[[179]](#cite_note-clra-182) The record high is 42.2 °C (108.0 °F) set on 14 April 1952,[[180]](#cite_note-IMDextremes-183) and the record low was 7.4 °C (45.3 °F), set on 27 January 1962.[[180]](#cite_note-IMDextremes-183)[[181]](#cite_note-184) The average annual [precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation_(meteorology) "Precipitation (meteorology)") is 2,213 mm (87.1 in).[[178]](#cite_note-Rohli_2007_267-181) The maximum annual rainfall ever recorded was 3,452 mm (136 in) for 1954.[[179]](#cite_note-clra-182) The [most rainfall](/wiki/2005_Mumbai_floods "2005 Mumbai floods") recorded in a single day was 944 mm (37 in) on 26 July 2005.[[182]](#cite_note-185) The average total annual rainfall is 2,213.4 mm (87 in) in Mumbai City and 2,502.3 mm (99 in) for the suburbs.[[179]](#cite_note-clra-182)

Mumbai encounters occasional [tropical cyclones](/wiki/Tropical_cyclone "Tropical cyclone"). The worst [cyclone](/wiki/Cyclone "Cyclone") encountered was on 23 November 1948, with gusts reaching 151 km/h (94 mph), and resulted in 38 deaths and 47 people missing.[[183]](#cite_note-186)[[184]](#cite_note-187)[[185]](#cite_note-188) The city is prone to [flooding](/wiki/Urban_flooding "Urban flooding") during monsoons,[[186]](#cite_note-189)[[187]](#cite_note-190) According to the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank"), unplanned drainage systems and [informal settlements](/wiki/Informal_settlement "Informal settlement") are amongst the key drivers of Mumbai's frequent floods.[[188]](#cite_note-WEF-191) The [2005 Mumbai floods](/wiki/2005_Mumbai_floods "2005 Mumbai floods") caused 500-1000 deaths and financial losses of US$ 1.2 billion.[[188]](#cite_note-WEF-191)[[189]](#cite_note-david,david&Janel-192) To reduce flooding in Mumbai, the [Maharashtra government](/wiki/Maharashtra_government "Maharashtra government") implemented a flood mitigation plan that included restoring the Mithi River, restructuring the drainage system, and moving informal settlements.[[188]](#cite_note-WEF-191)[[189]](#cite_note-david,david&Janel-192)[[190]](#cite_note-LSC-193)

### Pollution

[Air pollution](/wiki/Air_pollution_in_India "Air pollution in India") is a major issue in Mumbai.[[200]](#cite_note-203)[[201]](#cite_note-204)[[202]](#cite_note-205) According to the 2016 [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") Global Urban Ambient Air Pollution Database,[[203]](#cite_note-206) the annual average [PM2.5](/wiki/PM2.5 "PM2.5") concentration in 2013 was 63 μg/m3, which was 6.3 times higher than that recommended by the [WHO Air Quality Guideline](/wiki/WHO_Air_Quality_Guideline "WHO Air Quality Guideline") for annual mean PM2.5.[[204]](#cite_note-guidelines-207) The [Central Pollution Control Board](/wiki/Central_Pollution_Control_Board "Central Pollution Control Board") of the [Government of India](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India") monitors and publicly shares real-time air quality data.[[205]](#cite_note-208) In December 2019, the [Indian Institute of Technology Bombay](/wiki/Indian_Institute_of_Technology_Bombay "Indian Institute of Technology Bombay"), in partnership with the [McKelvey School of Engineering](/wiki/McKelvey_School_of_Engineering "McKelvey School of Engineering") at [Washington University in St. Louis](/wiki/Washington_University_in_St._Louis "Washington University in St. Louis"), launched the Aerosol and Air Quality Research Facility at Mumbai to study air pollution in Indian cities.[[206]](#cite_note-209) According to the 2016 report of the [Central Pollution Control Board](/wiki/Central_Pollution_Control_Board "Central Pollution Control Board"), Mumbai is the noisiest city in India.[[207]](#cite_note-210)

## Demographics

See also: [Growth of Mumbai](/wiki/Growth_of_Mumbai "Growth of Mumbai")

According to the [2011 census](/wiki/2011_census_of_India "2011 census of India"), Mumbai had a population of 12,442,373 inhabitants, with an estimated population density of about 20,482/km2 (53,050/sq mi) and averate living space of 4.5 square metres (48 sq ft) per person.[[209]](#cite_note-Census-212)[[210]](#cite_note-213) The Mumbai Metropolitan Region was home to 18,394,912 people in 2011.[[9]](#cite_note-extended_UA_2011-9) The [sex ratio](/wiki/Sex_ratio "Sex ratio") as 853 females per 1,000 males, lower than the national average of 914 females per 1,000 males. The child sex ratio was 913 females per 1000 males.[[209]](#cite_note-Census-212) The low sex ratio is partly because of the large number of male migrants who come to the city to work.[[211]](#cite_note-214) The city had a literacy rate of 89.73%, higher than the national average of 86.7%.[[209]](#cite_note-Census-212) There were 4.2 million households in 2008, which was projected to increase to 6.6 million by 2020. The number of households with annual incomes of above 2 million rupees was projected to be about 10%, and households with incomes from 1 to 2 million is estimated to be 15% in 2020.[[212]](#cite_note-215)

As per the 2011 census, there were 5,633,709 slum-dwellers living in 1,135,514 households.[[209]](#cite_note-Census-212)[[213]](#cite_note-216)[[214]](#cite_note-217)
Dharavi, located in central Mumbai, is the largest slum and houses about a million people in an area of 2.39 km2 (0.92 sq mi), making it one of the most densely populated areas on Earth.[[215]](#cite_note-218)[[216]](#cite_note-219)[[217]](#cite_note-Town-220) The number of migrants to Mumbai from outside Maharashtra during the 1991–2001 decade was 1.12 million, which amounted to 54.8% of the net addition to the population of Mumbai.[[218]](#cite_note-221)

### Ethnic groups and religions

Further information: [Religion in Maharashtra](/wiki/Religion_in_Maharashtra "Religion in Maharashtra")

As per the 2011 Census, [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") was the major religion with nearly 66% of the adherents, followed by [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") (20.65%), [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") (4.85%), [Jainism](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism") (4.10%), [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") (3.27%) and [Sikhisms](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism") (0.49%).[[209]](#cite_note-Census-212)[[219]](#cite_note-Religion-222) The oldest Muslim communities in Mumbai include the [Dawoodi Bohras](/wiki/Dawoodi_Bohra "Dawoodi Bohra"), Ismaili [Khojas](/wiki/Khojas "Khojas"), and [Konkani Muslims](/wiki/Konkani_Muslims "Konkani Muslims").[[220]](#cite_note-223) Native Christians include [East Indian Catholics](/wiki/Bombay_East_Indians "Bombay East Indians"), converted by the [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_India "Portuguese India") during the 16th century.[[221]](#cite_note-224)

[Jews settled in Mumbai](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Mumbai "History of the Jews in Mumbai") during the 18th century. The [Bene Israeli](/wiki/Bene_Israel "Bene Israel") Jewish community of Mumbai, who migrated from the [Konkan](/wiki/Konkan "Konkan") villages, south of Mumbai, are believed to be the descendants of the Jews of Israel shipwrecked off the Konkan coast, probably in the year 175 BCE, during the reign of the [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") ruler, [Antiochus IV Epiphanes](/wiki/Antiochus_IV_Epiphanes "Antiochus IV Epiphanes").[[222]](#cite_note-225) Mumbai is home to the largest population of [Parsi](/wiki/Parsi "Parsi") [Zoroastrians](/wiki/Zoroastrian "Zoroastrian") in the world,[[223]](#cite_note-226) numbering about 60,000, however their population is declining rapidly.[[224]](#cite_note-227) Parsis migrated to India from [Greater Iran](/wiki/Greater_Iran "Greater Iran") following the [Muslim conquest of Persia](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Persia "Muslim conquest of Persia") in the seventh century.[[225]](#cite_note-228) The ethnic demographics in the Mumbai consists of [Maharashtrians](/wiki/Maharashtrians "Maharashtrians") (32%), [Gujaratis](/wiki/Gujaratis "Gujaratis") (20%), with the rest hailing from other parts of India.[[226]](#cite_note-229)
