[Marathi](/wiki/Marathi_language "Marathi language") is the most spoken language, spoken by about 35.4% of the population. [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") is the second most spoken language, spoken by 24.8% of the population, followed by [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu") (11.7%) and [Gujarati](/wiki/Gujarati_language "Gujarati language") (11.4%).[[227]](#cite_note-census2011-langreport-230)[[229]](#cite_note-232)[[230]](#cite_note-51st_Report_CLMI-233) Marathi and English serve as the official languages of the government.[[230]](#cite_note-51st_Report_CLMI-233) English is the principal language of the city's [white collar](/wiki/White-collar_worker "White-collar worker") workforce. A colloquial form of Hindi, known as *[Bombay Hindi](/wiki/Bombay_Hindi "Bombay Hindi")* is spoken on the streets.[[231]](#cite_note-234) Many Hindi speakers are workers from [Uttar Pradesh](/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh "Uttar Pradesh") and [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar") who migrate seasonally to Mumbai to work as labourers.[[232]](#cite_note-235)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Mumbai](/wiki/Economy_of_Mumbai "Economy of Mumbai")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Lodha_Altamount_-_An_epitome_of_luxury.jpg/250px-Lodha_Altamount_-_An_epitome_of_luxury.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lodha_Altamount_-_An_epitome_of_luxury.jpg)

Skyscrapers on [Altamount Road](/wiki/Altamount_Road "Altamount Road")

Mumbai is known as the financial and commercial capital of India, generating about 6.16% of the country's total GDP.[[34]](#cite_note-mmrda_muip_gdp-35)[[149]](#cite_note-Mumbai_global-152)[[233]](#cite_note-The_Financial_Express-236) In 2023–24, it contributed to 19.8% of the GDP of the state of Maharashtra.[[234]](#cite_note-237) In 2017–18, estimates of the economy of the [Mumbai Metropolitan Region](/wiki/Mumbai_Metropolitan_Region "Mumbai Metropolitan Region") ranged from $368 billion to $400 billion ([PPP](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") & [metro GDP](/wiki/Gross_metropolitan_product "Gross metropolitan product")), ranking it amongst the top two most-productive [metro area](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_GDP "List of cities by GDP") of India.[[235]](#cite_note-Mumbai_GDP-238)[[236]](#cite_note-gqindia.com-239) In 2006, Mumbai employed 10% of the nation's factory workforce, contributed to 25% of the industrial output, 33% of the [income tax](/wiki/Income_tax "Income tax") collections, 60% of the [customs duty](/wiki/Customs_(tax) "Customs (tax)") collections, 20% of the central [excise tax](/wiki/Excise_tax "Excise tax") collections, 40% of the [foreign trade](/wiki/Foreign_trade "Foreign trade"), and generated ₹40 [billion](/wiki/1,000,000,000 "1,000,000,000") (equivalent to ₹130 billion or US$1.5 billion in 2023) in [corporate tax](/wiki/Corporate_tax "Corporate tax").[[237]](#cite_note-240)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Phiroze_Jeejeebhoy_Towers_Bombay_Stock_Exchange.jpg/250px-Phiroze_Jeejeebhoy_Towers_Bombay_Stock_Exchange.jpg)](/wiki/File:Phiroze_Jeejeebhoy_Towers_Bombay_Stock_Exchange.jpg)

The [Bombay Stock Exchange](/wiki/Bombay_Stock_Exchange "Bombay Stock Exchange") is the oldest in Asia

Until the 1970s, Mumbai owed its prosperity largely to [textile](/wiki/Textile "Textile") mills and [seaport](/wiki/Seaport "Seaport"), but the local economy has since then diversified to include [finance](/wiki/Finance "Finance"), [engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering"), diamond-polishing, [healthcare](/wiki/Healthcare "Healthcare"), and information technology.[[238]](#cite_note-241) It witnessed an economic boom since the [liberalisation of 1991](/wiki/Economic_liberalisation_in_India "Economic liberalisation in India"), with the finance sector booming in the mid-nineties, and the [information technology](/wiki/Information_technology "Information technology") (IT), export, services, and outsourcing booming in the 2000s.[[239]](#cite_note-242) In the 21st century. the key sectors contributing to the city's economy are: finance, gems & jewellery, leather processing, IT and [ITES](/wiki/Business_process_outsourcing "Business process outsourcing"), textiles, petrochemical, electronics manufacturing, automobiles, and entertainment.[[240]](#cite_note-ecoprofile-243) The port and shipping industry is well established.[[241]](#cite_note-244) [Dharavi](/wiki/Dharavi "Dharavi"), in central Mumbai, has a large recycling industry and is home to about 15,000 single-room factories.[[242]](#cite_note-gua-245)

The city is home to many of India's conglomerates and five of the [Fortune Global 500](/wiki/Fortune_Global_500 "Fortune Global 500") companies.[[149]](#cite_note-Mumbai_global-152) It also houses the [Reserve Bank of India](/wiki/Reserve_Bank_of_India "Reserve Bank of India") , the [Bombay Stock Exchange](/wiki/Bombay_Stock_Exchange "Bombay Stock Exchange") (BSE), the [National Stock Exchange of India](/wiki/National_Stock_Exchange_of_India "National Stock Exchange of India"), and financial sector regulators such as the [Securities and Exchange Board of India](/wiki/Securities_and_Exchange_Board_of_India "Securities and Exchange Board of India"). [Nariman Point](/wiki/Nariman_Point "Nariman Point") and [Bandra Kurla Complex](/wiki/Bandra_Kurla_Complex "Bandra Kurla Complex") are Mumbai's major financial centres.[[240]](#cite_note-ecoprofile-243) The BSE, established in 1875, is the oldest [stock exchange](/wiki/Stock_exchange "Stock exchange") in Asia.[[243]](#cite_note-246) In 2019, Mumbai was among the [world's top ten cities by number of billionaires](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_number_of_billionaires "List of cities by number of billionaires").[[244]](#cite_note-Mumbai_data-247) With a total wealth of around $960 billion,[[245]](#cite_note-248)[[246]](#cite_note-249) it is the wealthiest Indian city and one of the richest cities in the world.[[247]](#cite_note-Mumbai_info-250) As of 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Mumbai&action=edit), the [Globalization and World Cities Research Network](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network") has ranked Mumbai as an "[alpha](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network#Alpha "Globalization and World Cities Research Network") world city", third in its categories of [Global cities](/wiki/Global_city "Global city").[[20]](#cite_note-lboro2008-20) Mumbai is the third most expensive office market in the world, and has been ranked among the fastest cities in the country for business startup.[[248]](#cite_note-World_Bank_and_International_Financial_Corporation-251)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Dharavi_India.jpg/250px-Dharavi_India.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dharavi_India.jpg)

[Dharavi](/wiki/Dharavi "Dharavi") is one of the largest slums in the world

Mumbai is one of the world's most unequal cities in terms of wealth distribution and inclusivity.[[249]](#cite_note-252) According to the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank"), in 2010, the median income was ₹20,000 (equivalent to ₹45,000 or US$530 in 2023) while the average income was ₹40,000 (equivalent to ₹90,000 or US$1,100 in 2023).[[250]](#cite_note-253) The city has a large unskilled and semi-skilled self-employed population, and other [blue collar](/wiki/Blue_collar "Blue collar") professions, often migrating from other regions.[[251]](#cite_note-254)[[252]](#cite_note-255) About 41.8% of the city's population live in urban slums, though slums occupy just around seven percent of the city's total land area.[[253]](#cite_note-256)[[254]](#cite_note-Slum-257) Mumbai's overall per capita residential area is 8.3 m2 (89 sq ft), and in the city's slums, it drops to 2.73 m2 (29.4 sq ft).[[254]](#cite_note-Slum-257) Dharavi hosts nearly a million people, and is one of the largest slums in the world.[[255]](#cite_note-258) The median rental cost of a one-bedroom apartment in Mumbai proper was about ₹30,000 (equivalent to ₹37,000 or US$440 in 2023) in 2019.[[256]](#cite_note-Lewis-259) With available land at a premium, Mumbai residents often endure cramped housing, far from workplaces, with long commutes on crowded mass transits or roads clogged with traffic. Suburban residents spend a significant amount of time commuting southward to the central commercial district.[[257]](#cite_note-260)

## Government and politics

### Civic administration

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Municipal_Corporation_of_Greater_Mumbai_01.jpg/250px-Municipal_Corporation_of_Greater_Mumbai_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Municipal_Corporation_of_Greater_Mumbai_01.jpg)

Headquarters of the [Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation](/wiki/Brihanmumbai_Municipal_Corporation "Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation") (BMC), the largest civic organisation in the country.

Greater Mumbai (or Brihanmumbai), an area of 603 km2 (233 mi2),[[258]](#cite_note-261) consisting of the [Mumbai City](/wiki/Mumbai_City_district "Mumbai City district") and [Mumbai Suburban](/wiki/Mumbai_Suburban_district "Mumbai Suburban district") districts, extends from [Colaba](/wiki/Colaba "Colaba") in the south, to [Mulund](/wiki/Mulund "Mulund") and [Dahisar](/wiki/Dahisar "Dahisar") in the north, and [Mankhurd](/wiki/Mankhurd "Mankhurd") in the east. Its population as per the 2011 census was 12,442,373.[[259]](#cite_note-census2011-gtmumcity-262)

It is administered by the [Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation](/wiki/Brihanmumbai_Municipal_Corporation "Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation") (BMC) (also referred to as the [Municipal Corporation of Greater Mumbai](/wiki/Municipal_Corporation_of_Greater_Mumbai "Municipal Corporation of Greater Mumbai"), and formerly known as the Bombay Municipal Corporation).[[157]](#cite_note-mloc-160) The BMC is in charge of the civic and infrastructure needs of the metropolis.[[260]](#cite_note-mcmmm-263) BMC is the richest civic body in India and among the wealthiest in Asia.[[261]](#cite_note-264) As per the [Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Housing_and_Urban_Affairs "Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs"), the BMC had a revenue of ₹378.91 billion (US$4.5 billion) and an expenditure of ₹338.25 billion (US$4.0 billion) in 2022–23. Taxes contributed to 17.1% of the revenue, with 51.2% of the income coming from other sources, and 31.8% from government grants.[[262]](#cite_note-265)

The BMC is headed by a mayor, who serves for a term of two and a half years, is chosen through an [indirect election](/wiki/Indirect_election "Indirect election") by the councillors from among themselves.[[263]](#cite_note-266)[[264]](#cite_note-mayorterm-267) The [municipal commissioner](/wiki/Municipal_Commissioner_of_Mumbai "Municipal Commissioner of Mumbai") is the chief executive officer and head of the executive arm of the municipal corporation. The commissioner, who is an [Indian Administrative Service](/wiki/Indian_Administrative_Service "Indian Administrative Service") officer appointed by the [state government](/wiki/Government_of_Maharashtra "Government of Maharashtra"), is responsible for implementing the executive policies. The commissioner is appointed for a fixed term, and the powers of the commissioner are those provided by statute and those delegated by the corporation or the standing committee.[[265]](#cite_note-268)

As per a report in 2014, The Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation was ranked ninth out of 21 cities for best governance & administrative practices in India in 2014, scoring 3.5 out of 10, compared to the national average of 3.3.[[266]](#cite_note-269)

[![A brown building with a central tower and sloping roofs surrounded by trees. A grassy ground and a coconut tree are in front of it.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Mumbai_03-2016_41_Bombay_High_Court.jpg/250px-Mumbai_03-2016_41_Bombay_High_Court.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mumbai_03-2016_41_Bombay_High_Court.jpg)

The [Bombay High Court](/wiki/Bombay_High_Court "Bombay High Court") exercises jurisdiction over Maharashtra, [Goa](/wiki/Goa "Goa"), [Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu](/wiki/Dadra_and_Nagar_Haveli_and_Daman_and_Diu "Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu").

The [Mumbai Police](/wiki/Mumbai_Police "Mumbai Police") is headed by a [police commissioner](/wiki/Police_Commissioner_of_Mumbai "Police Commissioner of Mumbai"), who is an [Indian Police Service](/wiki/Indian_Police_Service "Indian Police Service") officer. The Mumbai Police is a division of the [Maharashtra Police](/wiki/Maharashtra_Police "Maharashtra Police"), under the home ministry of the state government.[[267]](#cite_note-270) The city is divided into seven police zones and seventeen [traffic police](/wiki/Mumbai_traffic_police "Mumbai traffic police") zones,[[268]](#cite_note-trf-271) each headed by a deputy commissioner of police.[[269]](#cite_note-272) The Mumbai Traffic Police is a semi-autonomous body under the Mumbai Police, and is responsible for traffic organisation in the city. The [Mumbai Fire Brigade](/wiki/Mumbai_Fire_Brigade "Mumbai Fire Brigade"), which is under the jurisdiction of the municipal corporation, is headed by the chief fire officer, who is assisted by four deputy chief fire officers and six divisional officers.[[268]](#cite_note-trf-271) The [Mumbai Metropolitan Region Development Authority](/wiki/Mumbai_Metropolitan_Region_Development_Authority "Mumbai Metropolitan Region Development Authority") is responsible for infrastructure development and planning of [Mumbai Metropolitan Region](/wiki/Mumbai_Metropolitan_Region "Mumbai Metropolitan Region").[[270]](#cite_note-273)

Mumbai is the seat of the [Bombay High Court](/wiki/Bombay_High_Court "Bombay High Court"), which exercises jurisdiction over the states of Maharashtra and [Goa](/wiki/Goa "Goa"), and the [Union Territory](/wiki/Union_Territory "Union Territory") of [Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu](/wiki/Dadra_and_Nagar_Haveli_and_Daman_and_Diu "Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu").[[271]](#cite_note-274) Mumbai also has two lower courts, the Court of Small Causes for civil matters, and the [Sessions Court](/wiki/Sessions_Court "Sessions Court") for criminal cases. Mumbai also has a special [Terrorist and Disruptive Activities](/wiki/Terrorist_and_Disruptive_Activities_(Prevention)_Act "Terrorist and Disruptive Activities (Prevention) Act") court for people accused of conspiring and abetting acts of terrorism in the city.[[272]](#cite_note-275)

### Politics

[![Men in traditional Indian dresses posing for a photograph](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/1st_INC1885.jpg/250px-1st_INC1885.jpg)](/wiki/File:1st_INC1885.jpg)

First session of the [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress") in Mumbai (28–31 December 1885)

The founding session of the [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress") (INC) was held in Mumbai in December 1885.[[273]](#cite_note-276) Its first session was held in Mumbai from 28 to 31 December 1885.[[274]](#cite_note-277) The city played host to the sessions of the INC six times during its first 50 years, and was a base for the [Indian independence movement](/wiki/Indian_independence_movement "Indian independence movement") during the 20th century.[[275]](#cite_note-278)

The 1960s saw the rise of regionalist politics, and the [Shiv Sena](/wiki/Shiv_Sena_(1966%E2%80%932022) "Shiv Sena (1966–2022)") was formed on 19 June 1966 by [Bal Thackeray](/wiki/Bal_Thackeray "Bal Thackeray") out of resentment about the marginalisation of [Marathi people](/wiki/Marathi_people "Marathi people") in Mumbai.[[276]](#cite_note-279) Shiv Sena switched from the 'Marathi cause' to a [Hindutva](/wiki/Hindutva "Hindutva") plank in 1985 and joined hands with [Bhartiya Janata Party](/wiki/Bhartiya_Janata_Party "Bhartiya Janata Party") (BJP) that year.[[277]](#cite_note-280) The Congress had dominated the politics of Mumbai from the Indian independence until the early 1980s, when the Shiv Sena won the 1985 municipal elections for the first time.[[278]](#cite_note-281) In 1989, the BJP forged an electoral alliance with the Shiv Sena to dislodge the Congress in the [Maharashtra Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Maharashtra_Legislative_Assembly "Maharashtra Legislative Assembly") elections. In 1999, several members left the Congress to form the [Nationalist Congress Party](/wiki/Nationalist_Congress_Party "Nationalist Congress Party") (NCP) but later allied with the Congress to form the [Democratic Front](/wiki/Democratic_Front_(India) "Democratic Front (India)").[[279]](#cite_note-282) Other parties such as [Maharashtra Navnirman Sena](/wiki/Maharashtra_Navnirman_Sena "Maharashtra Navnirman Sena"), [Samajwadi Party](/wiki/Samajwadi_Party "Samajwadi Party"), [Bahujan Samaj Party](/wiki/Bahujan_Samaj_Party "Bahujan Samaj Party"), [All India Majlis-e-Ittehadul Muslimeen](/wiki/All_India_Majlis-e-Ittehadul_Muslimeen "All India Majlis-e-Ittehadul Muslimeen") have contested and won seats in the local body elections in the city.[[280]](#cite_note-283)

In the [Indian national elections](/wiki/Elections_in_India "Elections in India") held every five years, Mumbai is represented by six parliamentary constituencies: [North](/wiki/Mumbai_North_(Lok_Sabha_constituency) "Mumbai North (Lok Sabha constituency)"), [North West](/wiki/Mumbai_North_West_(Lok_Sabha_constituency) "Mumbai North West (Lok Sabha constituency)"), [North East](/wiki/Mumbai_North_East_(Lok_Sabha_constituency) "Mumbai North East (Lok Sabha constituency)"), [North Central](/wiki/Mumbai_North_Central_(Lok_Sabha_constituency) "Mumbai North Central (Lok Sabha constituency)"), [South Central](/wiki/Mumbai_South_Central_(Lok_Sabha_constituency) "Mumbai South Central (Lok Sabha constituency)"), and [South](/wiki/Mumbai_South_(Lok_Sabha_constituency) "Mumbai South (Lok Sabha constituency)").[[281]](#cite_note-284) A [member of parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament,_Lok_Sabha "Member of Parliament, Lok Sabha") to the *[Lok Sabha](/wiki/Lok_Sabha "Lok Sabha")*, the lower house of the [Indian Parliament](/wiki/Indian_Parliament "Indian Parliament"), is elected from each of the parliamentary constituencies. In the [2019 national election](/wiki/2019_Indian_general_election "2019 Indian general election"), all six parliamentary constituencies were won by the BJP and Shiv Sena in alliance, each winning three seats.[[282]](#cite_note-285)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Vidhan_Bhavan_aerial_view.jpg/250px-Vidhan_Bhavan_aerial_view.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vidhan_Bhavan_aerial_view.jpg)

[Maharashtra Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Maharashtra_Legislative_Assembly "Maharashtra Legislative Assembly")

In the [Maharashtra state assembly](/wiki/Maharashtra_Legislative_Assembly "Maharashtra Legislative Assembly") elections held every five years, Mumbai is represented by 36 assembly constituencies.[[283]](#cite_note-286)[[284]](#cite_note-287) A [member of the legislative assembly](/wiki/Member_of_the_Legislative_Assembly_(India) "Member of the Legislative Assembly (India)") to the Maharashtra *[Vidhan Sabha](/wiki/Vidhan_Sabha "Vidhan Sabha")* (legislative assembly) is elected from each of the assembly constituencies. In the [2024 state assembly election](/wiki/2024_Maharashtra_Legislative_Assembly_election "2024 Maharashtra Legislative Assembly election"), out of the 36 constituencies, 15 were won by the BJP, 10 by [Shiv Sena (UBT)](/wiki/Shiv_Sena_(UBT) "Shiv Sena (UBT)"), six by the Shiv Sena, three by the Congress, and one each by the NCP and an independent.[[285]](#cite_note-288)

Elections are held every five years to elect the corporators of the BMC.[[286]](#cite_note-289) The Corporation comprises 227 directly elected Councillors representing the [24 municipal wards](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Mumbai "Administrative divisions of Mumbai"), five nominated Councillors with knowledge of municipal administration, and a ceremonial [mayor](/wiki/Mayor_of_Mumbai "Mayor of Mumbai").[[287]](#cite_note-290)[[288]](#cite_note-291)[[289]](#cite_note-292) In the [2026 municipal corporation elections](/wiki/2026_Brihanmumbai_Municipal_Corporation_election "2026 Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation election"), out of the 227 seats, the BJP-Shiv Sena alliance secured 118 seats, with the BJP winning 89 seats.[[290]](#cite_note-civic20102-293)

## Transport

Main article: [Transport in Mumbai](/wiki/Transport_in_Mumbai "Transport in Mumbai")

### Road

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Bandra_Worli_Seal_Link.jpg/250px-Bandra_Worli_Seal_Link.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bandra_Worli_Seal_Link.jpg)

The [Bandra-Worli Sea Link](/wiki/Bandra-Worli_Sea_Link "Bandra-Worli Sea Link") is amongst the [longest sea bridges in India](/wiki/List_of_longest_bridges_above_water_in_India "List of longest bridges above water in India")

Mumbai is one of the four termini of the [Golden Quadrilateral](/wiki/Golden_Quadrilateral "Golden Quadrilateral") highway system.[[291]](#cite_note-294)[[292]](#cite_note-295) The city is connected to [Chennai](/wiki/Chennai "Chennai") and [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi") by [National Highway 48](/wiki/National_Highway_48_(India) "National Highway 48 (India)"). The highways [NH-61](/wiki/National_Highway_61_(India) "National Highway 61 (India)") and [NH-66](/wiki/National_Highway_66_(India) "National Highway 66 (India)") originate in the outskirts of the city.[[293]](#cite_note-NH-296)[[294]](#cite_note-297) The [Mumbai–Pune Expressway](/wiki/Mumbai%E2%80%93Pune_Expressway "Mumbai–Pune Expressway") opened in 2000, and was the first [expressway](/wiki/Expressways_of_India "Expressways of India") in India.[[295]](#cite_note-298) The [Mumbai–Nagpur Expressway](/wiki/Mumbai%E2%80%93Nagpur_Expressway "Mumbai–Nagpur Expressway") opened in June 2025.[[296]](#cite_note-299)[[297]](#cite_note-300)[[298]](#cite_note-301) The [Delhi–Mumbai Expressway](/wiki/Delhi%E2%80%93Mumbai_Expressway "Delhi–Mumbai Expressway"),[[299]](#cite_note-302) is under construction from 2019, and is planned to be completed by 2027.[[300]](#cite_note-303)[[301]](#cite_note-304)[[302]](#cite_note-305) In 2022, the state government proposed the Konkan Expressway, traversing along the Konkan coast.[[303]](#cite_note-306)

Mumbai has approximately 1,900 km (1,200 mi) of public roads.[[304]](#cite_note-Autos-307) The [Eastern Freeway](/wiki/Eastern_Freeway_(Mumbai) "Eastern Freeway (Mumbai)"), which connects South Mumbai with Thane, opened in 2013.[[305]](#cite_note-308) The [Sion Panvel Expressway](/wiki/Sion_Panvel_Expressway "Sion Panvel Expressway") from [Sion](/wiki/Sion,_Mumbai "Sion, Mumbai") to [Panvel](/wiki/Panvel "Panvel") and the [Western Express Highway](/wiki/Western_Express_Highway "Western Express Highway") from [Bandra](/wiki/Bandra "Bandra") to [Bhayander](/wiki/Bhayander "Bhayander"), are the other major arterial roads in the city.[[306]](#cite_note-309) The [Bandra-Worli Sea Link](/wiki/Bandra-Worli_Sea_Link "Bandra-Worli Sea Link") bridge, along with [Mahim Causeway](/wiki/Mahim_Causeway "Mahim Causeway"), links South Mumbai to the city's western suburbs.[[307]](#cite_note-310) The 21.8 km (13.5 mi) long [Mumbai Trans Harbour Link](/wiki/Mumbai_Trans_Harbour_Link "Mumbai Trans Harbour Link") was inaugurated on 12 January 2024, and connects Mumbai with Navi Mumbai.[[308]](#cite_note-311) There are five [tolled](/wiki/Toll_plaza "Toll plaza") entry points to the city by road.[[309]](#cite_note-312)

Mumbai's transport system is one of the most congested in the world.[[310]](#cite_note-313)[[311]](#cite_note-314) As of March 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Mumbai&action=edit), Mumbai had about 721,000 private vehicles.[[312]](#cite_note-315) Black-and-yellow meter [taxis](/wiki/Taxicabs "Taxicabs"), and [auto rickshaws](/wiki/Auto_rickshaw "Auto rickshaw") serve are available for hire.[[313]](#cite_note-Bus-316) Auto rickshaws are allowed to operate only in suburban areas, while taxis are allowed to operate throughout the city, but work mostly in the City district.[[314]](#cite_note-Taxi-317) Taxis and the lower-cost auto rickshaws are legally required to run on [compressed natural gas](/wiki/Compressed_natural_gas "Compressed natural gas").[[314]](#cite_note-Taxi-317)[[315]](#cite_note-318) As of 2005[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Mumbai&action=edit), there were 56,459 black and yellow taxis [[316]](#cite_note-319) and 106,000 auto rickshaws, as of May 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Mumbai&action=edit).[[304]](#cite_note-Autos-307) Ride hailing services [Ola](/wiki/Ola_Consumer "Ola Consumer"), [Rapido](/wiki/Rapido_(company) "Rapido (company)"), and [Uber](/wiki/Uber "Uber") operate in the city.[[317]](#cite_note-320)

#### Bus

[![A BEST bus on Route 56](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Mumbai-BEST-Kinglong-Bus.jpg/250px-Mumbai-BEST-Kinglong-Bus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mumbai-BEST-Kinglong-Bus.jpg)

A public bus run by [Brihanmumbai Electric Supply and Transport](/wiki/Brihanmumbai_Electric_Supply_and_Transport "Brihanmumbai Electric Supply and Transport")

The public bus transport system is operated by [Brihanmumbai Electric Supply and Transport](/wiki/Brihanmumbai_Electric_Supply_and_Transport "Brihanmumbai Electric Supply and Transport") (BEST). Buses carried over 5.5 million passengers per day in 2008, and the BEST bus services together with the Mumbai's local rail network accounted for about 88% of the passenger traffic.[[313]](#cite_note-Bus-316) The bus ridership dropped to 2.8 million in 2015.[[318]](#cite_note-321) Buses are favoured for commuting short to medium distances.[[319]](#cite_note-322) Public buses cover the entire city, and extend to parts of [Mira-Bhayandar](/wiki/Mira-Bhayandar "Mira-Bhayandar"), Navi Mumbai, and Thane.[[320]](#cite_note-323) As of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Mumbai&action=edit), the BEST operated 4,608 buses along 390 routes.[[313]](#cite_note-Bus-316)[[321]](#cite_note-324) The fleet consists of single-decker, double-decker, vestibule, low-floor, and air-conditioned buses, and run on diesel, compressed natural gas, or [electric](/wiki/Electric_bus "Electric bus") power.[[322]](#cite_note-325)[[323]](#cite_note-326) The earlier BEST buses were red, and based on London's [Routemaster](/wiki/AEC_Routemaster "AEC Routemaster") buses.[[324]](#cite_note-327) BEST introduced air-conditioned buses in 1998.[[325]](#cite_note-328)

[Maharashtra State Road Transport Corporation](/wiki/Maharashtra_State_Road_Transport_Corporation "Maharashtra State Road Transport Corporation") buses provide intercity transport connecting Mumbai with other cities of Maharashtra and nearby states.[[326]](#cite_note-329)[[327]](#cite_note-330)[[328]](#cite_note-331) The [Navi Mumbai Municipal Transport](/wiki/Navi_Mumbai_Municipal_Transport "Navi Mumbai Municipal Transport") and [Thane Municipal Transport](/wiki/Thane_Municipal_Transport "Thane Municipal Transport") operate buses in Mumbai, connecting Navi Mumbai and Thane to parts of Mumbai.[[329]](#cite_note-332)[[330]](#cite_note-333) The *Mumbai Darshan* tourist bus service covers the various [tourist attractions in the city](/wiki/List_of_tourist_attractions_in_Mumbai "List of tourist attractions in Mumbai").[[331]](#cite_note-334) Bus Rapid Transit System lanes have been planned throughout Mumbai.[[332]](#cite_note-335)

### Rail

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Rapid_transit_map_of_Mumbai.jpg/250px-Rapid_transit_map_of_Mumbai.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rapid_transit_map_of_Mumbai.jpg)

Rail map of Mumbai

The [Mumbai Suburban Railway](/wiki/Mumbai_Suburban_Railway "Mumbai Suburban Railway"), colloquially referred to as "locals", forms the backbone of the city's transport system.[[333]](#cite_note-Outlook-336) It is operated by the [Central](/wiki/Central_Railway_zone "Central Railway zone") and [Western Railway zones](/wiki/Western_Railway_zone "Western Railway zone") of the [Indian Railways](/wiki/Indian_Railways "Indian Railways").[[334]](#cite_note-337) Mumbai's suburban rail systems carried 6.3 million passengers per day in 2007.[[335]](#cite_note-338) The suburban rail network spans 319 km (198 mi), and 2,226 train services operated daily in 2010.[[336]](#cite_note-339) The trains are often overcrowded during peak hours, with twelve-car trains with a rated capacity 1,700 passengers, carrying nearly 4,500 passengers.[[337]](#cite_note-340) The [Mumbai Monorail](/wiki/Mumbai_Monorail "Mumbai Monorail") and [Mumbai Metro](/wiki/Mumbai_Metro "Mumbai Metro") were inaugurated to relieve the overcrowding on the existing rail network. The Monorail operates on a single route and opened in early February 2014.[[338]](#cite_note-341) The [first line](/wiki/Line_1_(Mumbai_Metro) "Line 1 (Mumbai Metro)") of the Mumbai Metro opened in June 2014.[[339]](#cite_note-342) After that, many lines of Mumbai Metro have started operating partially or fully.

Mumbai is the headquarters of two zones of the [Indian Railways](/wiki/Indian_Railways "Indian Railways"): the Central Railway headquartered at [Chhatrapati Shivaji Terminus](/wiki/Chhatrapati_Shivaji_Terminus "Chhatrapati Shivaji Terminus"), and the Western Railway headquartered at [Churchgate](/wiki/Churchgate "Churchgate").[[340]](#cite_note-343) Mumbai is well connected with major stations across the Indian Railways network. Long-distance trains operate from Chhatrapati Shivaji Terminus, [Dadar](/wiki/Dadar_railway_station "Dadar railway station"), [Lokmanya Tilak Terminus](/wiki/Lokmanya_Tilak_Terminus "Lokmanya Tilak Terminus"), [Mumbai Central](/wiki/Mumbai_Central "Mumbai Central"), and [Bandra Terminus](/wiki/Bandra_Terminus "Bandra Terminus") railway stations.[[341]](#cite_note-344) As of 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Mumbai&action=edit), four lines of the metro are operational and eight are planned.[[342]](#cite_note-345)

### Air

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/MumbaiAirportT2.JPG/250px-MumbaiAirportT2.JPG)](/wiki/File:MumbaiAirportT2.JPG)

[Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj International Airport](/wiki/Chhatrapati_Shivaji_Maharaj_International_Airport "Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj International Airport") is the second busiest airport in India

The [Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj International Airport](/wiki/Chhatrapati_Shivaji_Maharaj_International_Airport "Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj International Airport") is the main aviation hub in the city. It handled 52.8 million passengers in 2023–24, and is the second busiest airport in India in terms of passenger traffic.[[343]](#cite_note-346)[[344]](#cite_note-347) A modernisation and upgrade plan was initiated in 2006, targeted at increasing the capacity of the airport to handle up to 40 million passengers annually,[[345]](#cite_note-348) and the new T2 terminal was opened in February 2014.[[346]](#cite_note-349)

The [Navi Mumbai International airport](/wiki/Navi_Mumbai_International_airport "Navi Mumbai International airport"), built in the [Ulwe](/wiki/Ulwe "Ulwe") suburb in Raigad district, opened in December 2025, and serves as the second international airport in the metropolitan region.[[347]](#cite_note-350)[[348]](#cite_note-351) The [Juhu Aerodrome](/wiki/Juhu_Aerodrome "Juhu Aerodrome") hosts the [Bombay Flying Club](/wiki/Bombay_Flying_Club "Bombay Flying Club"), a [heliport](/wiki/Heliport "Heliport") operated by state-owned [Pawan Hans](/wiki/Pawan_Hans "Pawan Hans"), and handles private flights.[[349]](#cite_note-352)

### Water

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Jawaharlal_Nehru_Trust_Port.jpg/250px-Jawaharlal_Nehru_Trust_Port.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jawaharlal_Nehru_Trust_Port.jpg)

[Jawaharlal Nehru Port](/wiki/Jawaharlal_Nehru_Port "Jawaharlal Nehru Port") is the busiest port in India

Main article: [Water transport in Mumbai](/wiki/Water_transport_in_Mumbai "Water transport in Mumbai")

Mumbai is served by two major ports, [Mumbai Port](/wiki/Mumbai_Port "Mumbai Port") and [Jawaharlal Nehru Port](/wiki/Jawaharlal_Nehru_Port "Jawaharlal Nehru Port"), which lies just across the creek in Navi Mumbai.[[350]](#cite_note-353) Mumbai Port is a natural harbour, and has extensive wet and dry dock accommodation facilities.[[351]](#cite_note-354) Jawaharlal Nehru Port, commissioned on 26 May 1989, is the busiest major port in India,[[352]](#cite_note-355) and handles around 50% of the country's total containerised cargo.[[353]](#cite_note-356)

Local water transport in Mumbai consists of ferries, small boats, and catamarans. Ferry services are operated by government agencies and private partners.[[354]](#cite_note-357) [Hovercraft](/wiki/Hovercraft "Hovercraft") services plied briefly in the late 1990s between the [Gateway of India](/wiki/Gateway_of_India "Gateway of India") and [CBD Belapur](/wiki/CBD_Belapur "CBD Belapur") in Navi Mumbai, and were scrapped due to lack of adequate [infrastructure](/wiki/Water_transport_infrastructure "Water transport infrastructure") and opposition from the local fishermen.[[355]](#cite_note-358) Ferries from [Ferry Wharf](/wiki/Ferry_Wharf "Ferry Wharf") in [Mazagaon](/wiki/Mazagaon "Mazagaon") allow access to islands near the city.[[356]](#cite_note-359)

The city is also the headquarters of the [Western Naval Command](/wiki/Western_Naval_Command "Western Naval Command"), and an important base for the [Indian Navy](/wiki/Indian_Navy "Indian Navy").[[157]](#cite_note-mloc-160)

## Utility services

See also: [Water sources of Mumbai](/wiki/Water_sources_of_Mumbai "Water sources of Mumbai")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Eastern_Express_Highway_Mumbai_May_2023.jpg/250px-Eastern_Express_Highway_Mumbai_May_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eastern_Express_Highway_Mumbai_May_2023.jpg)

[Deonar dumping ground](/wiki/Deonar_dumping_ground "Deonar dumping ground") along the [Eastern Express Highway](/wiki/Eastern_Express_Highway "Eastern Express Highway")

The municipal corporation supplies potable water to the city drawn from six lakes and water bodies in and around the city.[[357]](#cite_note-360)[[358]](#cite_note-361) The Tansa lake supplies water to the western suburbs and parts of the City district.[[359]](#cite_note-362) The water drawn from the lake is filtered at [Bhandup](/wiki/Bhandup "Bhandup"), which has the Asia's largest water filtration plant.[[360]](#cite_note-363)[[361]](#cite_note-364)[[362]](#cite_note-365) The water is supplied to the filtering facility using underground water tunnels.[[363]](#cite_note-366)[[364]](#cite_note-367) As of 2009[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Mumbai&action=edit), the city required a daily supply of 3.5 billion litres of water, of which 700 million litres is lost by way of water thefts, illegal connections and leakages.[[365]](#cite_note-368)

As of 2005[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Mumbai&action=edit), the city generated a refuse of 7,800 t (7,700 long tons; 8,600 short tons), of which 40 t (39 long tons; 44 short tons) is [plastic waste](/wiki/Plastic_pollution "Plastic pollution"),[[366]](#cite_note-369) and is transported to dumping grounds in [Gorai](/wiki/Gorai "Gorai") in the northwest, [Mulund](/wiki/Mulund "Mulund") in the northeast, and to the [Deonar](/wiki/Deonar_dumping_ground "Deonar dumping ground") in the east.[[367]](#cite_note-370) Sewage treatment is carried out at [Worli](/wiki/Worli "Worli") and [Bandra](/wiki/Bandra "Bandra"), and disposed of by two independent marine outfalls of 3.4 km (2.1 mi) and 3.7 km (2.3 mi) at Bandra and Worli respectively.[[368]](#cite_note-371)

Electricity is distributed by BEST in the City district, and by Maharashtra State Electricity Distribution Company ([Mahavitaran](/wiki/Mahavitaran "Mahavitaran")), [Adani Transmission](/wiki/Adani_Transmission "Adani Transmission"), and [Tata Power](/wiki/Tata_Power "Tata Power") in the suburbs.[[369]](#cite_note-372)[[370]](#cite_note-373) Majority of the power supply cables are [underground](/wiki/Undergrounding "Undergrounding"), to reduce pilferage, thefts and other losses.[[371]](#cite_note-374)[[372]](#cite_note-375) Cooking gas is supplied in the form of [Liquefied petroleum gas](/wiki/Liquefied_petroleum_gas "Liquefied petroleum gas") cylinders sold by various state-owned oil companies, and pieped [natural gas](/wiki/Liquefied_natural_gas "Liquefied natural gas") supplied by Mahanagar Gas Limited.[[373]](#cite_note-376)[[374]](#cite_note-377)

The state-owned [MTNL](/wiki/MTNL "MTNL"), which held a monopoly over fixed line and cellular services up until 2000, and provides fixed line as well as mobile [WLL](/wiki/Wireless_local_loop "Wireless local loop") services.[[375]](#cite_note-378) Mobile phone coverage is extensive, and the main service providers include [Bharti Airtel](/wiki/Bharti_Airtel "Bharti Airtel"), [Reliance Jio](/wiki/Jio "Jio"), MTNL, and [Vodafone Idea](/wiki/Vodafone_Idea "Vodafone Idea").[[376]](#cite_note-379) Mumbai, along with the area served by [telephone exchanges](/wiki/Telephone_exchange "Telephone exchange") in Navi Mumbai and [Kalyan](/wiki/Kalyan "Kalyan") is classified as a metro telecom circle.[[377]](#cite_note-380) Various operators provide broadband internet and wireless internet access, and as of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Mumbai&action=edit), Mumbai had the highest number of internet users in India with 16.4 million users.[[378]](#cite_note-381)

## Education

See also: [List of educational institutions in Mumbai § Schools](/wiki/List_of_educational_institutions_in_Mumbai#Schools "List of educational institutions in Mumbai"), and [List of colleges in Mumbai](/wiki/List_of_colleges_in_Mumbai "List of colleges in Mumbai")

### Schools

Schools in Mumbai include municipal schools run by the municipal corporation, government aided, and private schools.[[379]](#cite_note-382) The schools usually use Marathi or English as the primary language of instruction.[[380]](#cite_note-383) As of 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Mumbai&action=edit), the BMC operated 1,135 schools which provided free education to nearly 293,000 students. The schools taught in eight languages (Marathi, Hindu, Gujarati, Urdu, English, [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language"), [Telugu](/wiki/Telugu_language "Telugu language") and [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada_language "Kannada language")).[[381]](#cite_note-384)[[382]](#cite_note-385)

### Higher education

Under the [10+2+3 plan](/wiki/10%2B2%2B3_plan "10+2+3 plan"), students complete ten years of schooling and then two years in [junior college](/wiki/Junior_college "Junior college"), where they select one of three streams: arts, commerce, or science.[[383]](#cite_note-386) This is followed by either a degree course in any of three disciplines, or a professional degree course, such as engineering, law, and medicine.[[384]](#cite_note-387)

Most colleges are affiliated with the [University of Mumbai](/wiki/University_of_Mumbai "University of Mumbai"), established in 1857, and is one of the premier universities in India.[[385]](#cite_note-Old-388)[[386]](#cite_note-389) It was ranked 41 among the top 50 engineering schools by *[Business Insider](/wiki/Business_Insider "Business Insider")* in 2012,[[387]](#cite_note-390) fifth in the list of best universities in India by *[India Today](/wiki/India_Today "India Today")* in 2013,[[388]](#cite_note-391) and 10th among the top Universities of India in 2013 in the [QS World University Rankings](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings").[[389]](#cite_note-392)[[390]](#cite_note-393) The [Wilson College](/wiki/Wilson_College,_Mumbai "Wilson College, Mumbai") was the first college in the city, established in 1832. The [Grant Government Medical College](/wiki/Grant_Medical_College_and_Sir_JJ_Group_of_Hospitals "Grant Medical College and Sir JJ Group of Hospitals") was opened in 1845, the [Government Law College](/wiki/Government_Law_College,_Mumbai "Government Law College, Mumbai") in 1855, and [Elphinstone College](/wiki/Elphinstone_College "Elphinstone College") in 1856. The [Veermata Jijabai Technological Institute](/wiki/Veermata_Jijabai_Technological_Institute "Veermata Jijabai Technological Institute"), was the first engineering institute, opened in 1887.[[385]](#cite_note-Old-388)

The [Indian Institute of Technology Bombay](/wiki/IIT_Bombay "IIT Bombay"),[[391]](#cite_note-394) and [Institute of Chemical Technology](/wiki/Institute_of_Chemical_Technology "Institute of Chemical Technology") are amongst the major engineering schools in the city.[[392]](#cite_note-395) Mumbai is home to several prominent business schools including [IIM Mumbai](/wiki/Indian_Institute_of_Management_Mumbai "Indian Institute of Management Mumbai"), [Jamnalal Bajaj Institute of Management Studies](/wiki/Jamnalal_Bajaj_Institute_of_Management_Studies "Jamnalal Bajaj Institute of Management Studies"), [Narsee Monjee Institute of Management Studies](/wiki/Narsee_Monjee_Institute_of_Management_Studies "Narsee Monjee Institute of Management Studies"), [S P Jain Institute of Management and Research](/wiki/S_P_Jain_Institute_of_Management_and_Research "S P Jain Institute of Management and Research"), and [Tata Institute of Social Sciences](/wiki/Tata_Institute_of_Social_Sciences "Tata Institute of Social Sciences").[[393]](#cite_note-396)[[394]](#cite_note-397)[[395]](#cite_note-398) The [Sir J. J. School of Art](/wiki/Sir_J._J._School_of_Art "Sir J. J. School of Art") is Mumbai's oldest art institution.[[396]](#cite_note-399) The city is home to the [Tata Institute of Fundamental Research](/wiki/Tata_Institute_of_Fundamental_Research "Tata Institute of Fundamental Research"), and the [Bhabha Atomic Research Centre](/wiki/Bhabha_Atomic_Research_Centre "Bhabha Atomic Research Centre"), which operates [CIRUS](/wiki/CIRUS "CIRUS") nuclear research reactor in [Trombay](/wiki/Trombay "Trombay").[[397]](#cite_note-400)[[398]](#cite_note-401)

## Culture

Main article: [Mumbai culture](/wiki/Mumbai_culture "Mumbai culture")

[![A white building with a triangular façade and wide stairs](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Mumbai_03-2016_70_Asiatic_Society_Library.jpg/250px-Mumbai_03-2016_70_Asiatic_Society_Library.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mumbai_03-2016_70_Asiatic_Society_Library.jpg)

[The Asiatic Society of Mumbai](/wiki/The_Asiatic_Society_of_Mumbai "The Asiatic Society of Mumbai") is one of the oldest [public libraries](/wiki/Public_library "Public library") in the city.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Chowpatti_Beach_%28Girgaum_Chowpatty%29.jpg/250px-Chowpatti_Beach_%28Girgaum_Chowpatty%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chowpatti_Beach_(Girgaum_Chowpatty).jpg)

[Girgaum Chowpatty](/wiki/Girgaum_Chowpatty "Girgaum Chowpatty") beach. Beaches are a popular tourist attraction in the city.

Mumbai's culture offers a blend of traditional and cosmopolitan festivals, food, entertainment, and night life. Many cultures, religions, and cuisines coexist in the city, producing varied restaurants, cinemas, theatres, sports events and museums.[[399]](#cite_note-402)

Mumbai is the birthplace of [Indian cinema](/wiki/Indian_cinema "Indian cinema").[[400]](#cite_note-403)[[401]](#cite_note-404) It has many cinemas that feature Bollywood, Marathi and Hollywood movies. The [Mumbai International Film Festival](/wiki/Mumbai_International_Film_Festival "Mumbai International Film Festival")[[402]](#cite_note-405) and the award ceremony of the [Filmfare Awards](/wiki/Filmfare_Awards "Filmfare Awards"), the oldest and prominent film awards given for Hindi film industry in India, are held in Mumbai.[[403]](#cite_note-406) Despite most of the professional theatre groups that formed during the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") having disbanded by the 1950s, Mumbai has developed a thriving "theatre movement" tradition in Marathi, Hindi, English, and other regional languages.[[404]](#cite_note-multitheater-407)[[405]](#cite_note-408)

Contemporary art is featured in both government-funded art spaces and private commercial galleries. The government-funded institutions include the [Jehangir Art Gallery](/wiki/Jehangir_Art_Gallery "Jehangir Art Gallery") and the [National Gallery of Modern Art](/wiki/National_Gallery_of_Modern_Art "National Gallery of Modern Art"). Built in 1833, the [Asiatic Society of Mumbai](/wiki/Asiatic_Society_of_Bombay "Asiatic Society of Bombay") is one of the oldest [public libraries](/wiki/Public_library "Public library") in the city.[[406]](#cite_note-409) The [Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj Vastu Sangrahalaya](/wiki/Chhatrapati_Shivaji_Maharaj_Vastu_Sangrahalaya "Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj Vastu Sangrahalaya") (formerly The Prince of Wales Museum) is a renowned museum in [South Mumbai](/wiki/South_Mumbai "South Mumbai") which houses rare ancient exhibits of Indian history.[[407]](#cite_note-410)

Mumbai has a zoo named [Jijamata Udyaan](/wiki/Jijamata_Udyaan "Jijamata Udyaan") (formerly Victoria Gardens), which also harbours a garden. The literary traditions of the city have been highlighted internationally by [Booker Prize](/wiki/Booker_Prize "Booker Prize") winners such as [Salman Rushdie](/wiki/Salman_Rushdie "Salman Rushdie") and [Aravind Adiga](/wiki/Aravind_Adiga "Aravind Adiga"). [Marathi literature](/wiki/Marathi_literature "Marathi literature") has been modernised in the works of Mumbai-based authors such as Mohan Apte, [Anant Kanekar](/wiki/Anant_Kanekar "Anant Kanekar"), and [Gangadhar Gadgil](/wiki/Gangadhar_Gadgil "Gangadhar Gadgil"), and is promoted through an annual [Sahitya Akademi Award](/wiki/Sahitya_Akademi_Award "Sahitya Akademi Award"), a literary honour bestowed by India's [National Academy of Letters](/wiki/Sahitya_Akademi "Sahitya Akademi").[[408]](#cite_note-411)

Mumbai residents celebrate both Western and [Indian festivals](/wiki/Indian_festivals "Indian festivals"). [Ganesh Chaturthi](/wiki/Ganesh_Chaturthi "Ganesh Chaturthi") is the biggest and most important festival of Mumbai; there are almost 5000 [Ganpati](/wiki/Ganpati "Ganpati") Pandals set up in the city for the celebrations. Other festivals like [Diwali](/wiki/Diwali "Diwali"), [Holi](/wiki/Holi "Holi"), [Navratri](/wiki/Navratri "Navratri"), [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas"), [Rakshabandhan](/wiki/Rakshabandhan "Rakshabandhan"), [Makar Sankranti](/wiki/Makar_Sankranti "Makar Sankranti"), [Dussera](/wiki/Navrati "Navrati"), [Eid](/wiki/Muslim_holidays "Muslim holidays"), [Durga Puja](/wiki/Durga_Puja "Durga Puja"), [Ram Navami](/wiki/Ram_Navami "Ram Navami"), [Shiv Jayanti](/wiki/Shiv_Jayanti "Shiv Jayanti") and [Maha Shivratri](/wiki/Maha_Shivratri "Maha Shivratri") are some of the popular festivals in the city. The [Kala Ghoda Arts Festival](/wiki/Kala_Ghoda_Arts_Festival "Kala Ghoda Arts Festival") is an exhibition that encapsulates works of artists in the fields of music, dance, theatre and films.[[409]](#cite_note-412)

The Banganga Festival is a two-day music festival, held annually in the month of January, which is organised by the [Maharashtra Tourism Development Corporation](/wiki/Maharashtra_Tourism_Development_Corporation "Maharashtra Tourism Development Corporation") (MTDC) at the historic [Banganga Tank](/wiki/Banganga_Tank "Banganga Tank") in Mumbai. According to a legend, it is associated with [Rama](/wiki/Rama "Rama") and [Lakshmana](/wiki/Lakshmana "Lakshmana") of the Sanskrit epic [Ramayana](/wiki/Ramayana "Ramayana").[[410]](#cite_note-413) Similarly it is also linked to [Parashurama](/wiki/Parashurama "Parashurama") in Hinduism. On the bank of the Banganga tank, there is a temple known as [Parshuram Mandir](/wiki/Parshuram_Mandir,_Walkeshwar "Parshuram Mandir, Walkeshwar") dedicated to Parshuram.[[411]](#cite_note-MTDCpullout-414)[[412]](#cite_note-MTDCBanganga-415)[[413]](#cite_note-416)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Parshuram_Mandir%2C_Walkeshwar.jpg/250px-Parshuram_Mandir%2C_Walkeshwar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parshuram_Mandir,_Walkeshwar.jpg)

View of [Parshuram Mandir](/wiki/Parshuram_Mandir,_Walkeshwar "Parshuram Mandir, Walkeshwar") from the Banganga tank in the Walkeshwar area of Malabar Hill in South Mumbai

The Elephanta Festival—celebrated every February on the [Elephanta Islands](/wiki/Elephanta_Island "Elephanta Island")—is dedicated to classical Indian dance and music and attracts performers from across the country.[[411]](#cite_note-MTDCpullout-414)[[414]](#cite_note-MTDCElephanta-417) Public holidays specific to the city and the state include [Maharashtra Day](/wiki/Maharashtra_Day "Maharashtra Day") on 1 May, to celebrate the formation of Maharashtra state on 1 May 1960,[[415]](#cite_note-418)[[416]](#cite_note-419) and [Gudi Padwa](/wiki/Gudi_Padwa "Gudi Padwa") which is the New Year's Day for [Marathi people](/wiki/Marathi_people "Marathi people").

[Beaches](/wiki/List_of_Mumbai_beaches "List of Mumbai beaches") are a major tourist attraction in the city. The major beaches in Mumbai are [Girgaum Chowpatty](/wiki/Girgaum_Chowpatty "Girgaum Chowpatty"), [Juhu Beach](/wiki/Juhu_Beach "Juhu Beach"), Dadar Chowpatty, Gorai Beach, [Marve Beach](/wiki/Marve_Beach "Marve Beach"), Versova Beach, Madh Beach, [Aksa Beach](/wiki/Aksa_Beach "Aksa Beach") and [Manori](/wiki/Manori "Manori") Beach.[[417]](#cite_note-420) Most of the beaches are unfit for swimming, except Girgaum Chowpatty and Juhu Beach.[[418]](#cite_note-421) Essel World is a theme park and amusement centre situated close to Gorai Beach,[[419]](#cite_note-422) and includes Asia's largest theme water park, Water Kingdom.[[420]](#cite_note-423) [Adlabs Imagica](/wiki/Adlabs_Imagica "Adlabs Imagica") opened in April 2013 is located near the city of Khopoli off the [Mumbai-Pune Expressway](/wiki/Mumbai-Pune_Expressway "Mumbai-Pune Expressway").[[421]](#cite_note-founderimagica-424)

## Architecture

Main article: [Architecture of Mumbai](/wiki/Architecture_of_Mumbai "Architecture of Mumbai")

See also: [Heritage structures in Mumbai](/wiki/Heritage_structures_in_Mumbai "Heritage structures in Mumbai") and [List of tallest buildings in Mumbai](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Mumbai "List of tallest buildings in Mumbai")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Mumbaicityskyline.jpeg/250px-Mumbaicityskyline.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Mumbaicityskyline.jpeg)

The skyline of [Worli](/wiki/Worli "Worli") and [Dadar](/wiki/Dadar "Dadar") viewed from [Bandra](/wiki/Bandra "Bandra")

The architecture of the city is a blend of [Gothic Revival](/wiki/Gothic_Revival "Gothic Revival"), [Indo-Saracenic](/wiki/Indo-Saracenic "Indo-Saracenic"), [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco"), and other contemporary styles. Most of the buildings during the British period, such as the [Victoria Terminus](/wiki/Victoria_Terminus "Victoria Terminus") and [University of Mumbai](/wiki/University_of_Mumbai "University of Mumbai"), were built in Gothic Revival style.[[422]](#cite_note-425) Their architectural features include a variety of European influences such as German gables, Dutch roofs, Swiss timbering, Romance arches, Tudor casements, and traditional Indian features.[[423]](#cite_note-Profile-426) There are also a few Indo-Saracenic styled buildings such as the [Gateway of India](/wiki/Gateway_of_India "Gateway of India").[[424]](#cite_note-427) Art Deco styled landmarks can be found along [Marine Drive](/wiki/Marine_Drive,_Mumbai "Marine Drive, Mumbai") and west of the [Oval Maidan](/wiki/Oval_Maidan "Oval Maidan"). Mumbai has the second highest number of Art Deco buildings in the world after [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami"). In the newer suburbs, modern buildings dominate the landscape. Mumbai has by far the highest number of [skyscrapers](/wiki/Skyscrapers "Skyscrapers") in India, with 956 existing [skyscrapers](/wiki/Skyscrapers "Skyscrapers") and 272 under construction as of 2009[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Mumbai&action=edit).
The Mumbai Heritage Conservation Committee (MHCC), established in 1995, formulates special regulations and by-laws to assist in the conservation of the city's heritage structures. Mumbai has three [UNESCO World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Sites "World Heritage Sites"), the Chhatrapati Shivaji Terminus, the [Elephanta Caves](/wiki/Elephanta_Caves "Elephanta Caves") and the [Victorian and Art Deco Ensemble](/wiki/The_Victorian_and_Art_Deco_Ensemble_of_Mumbai "The Victorian and Art Deco Ensemble of Mumbai").[[425]](#cite_note-428) In the south of Mumbai, there are colonial-era buildings and Soviet-style offices.[[426]](#cite_note-economist-429) In the east are factories and some slums. On the West coast are former-textile mills being demolished and skyscrapers built on top. There are 237 buildings taller than 100 m (330 ft), compared with 327 in Shanghai and 855 in New York.[[427]](#cite_note-430)[[426]](#cite_note-economist-429)

### Cityscape

Mumbai's cityscape consists of a variety of tall buildings and structures, most of which have been built in the last two decades. There was a lull in construction from the mid-1990s, after which construction projects began taking the skyline upwards, with a major acceleration since 2000, when the [Lower Parel](/wiki/Parel "Parel") area began developing.[[428]](#cite_note-431)
Mumbai, with a commanding 77% share of tall buildings in India, is poised to maintain its position as the frontrunner in tall building construction due to its ability to command premium prices compared to other cities.[[429]](#cite_note-432)
Mumbai has more residential tall buildings rather than commercial, unlike the trend globally. Limited land resources and an exponential increase in urban population were the primary reasons for Mumbai's vertical growth compared to other Tier 1 Indian cities.[[430]](#cite_note-433) As of June 2023, Mumbai has a total of around 250 tall buildings out of which more than 100 are completed, and more than 90 were under construction.[[431]](#cite_note-434)

## Food

This section is an excerpt from [Street food of Mumbai](/wiki/Street_food_of_Mumbai "Street food of Mumbai").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Street_food_of_Mumbai&action=edit)]

[Street food of Mumbai](/wiki/Street_food_of_Mumbai "Street food of Mumbai") is the food sold by hawkers from portable market stalls in Mumbai. Although street food is common all over India, Mumbai is known for their street food and it's a strong characteristic of the city.[[432]](#cite_note-Street_food_of_Mumbai_da04-435)[[433]](#cite_note-Street_food_of_Mumbai_jg07-436) It has this reputation because people from all [economic classes](/wiki/Economic_class "Economic class") eat on the roadside almost round the clock, and it is sometimes felt that the taste of street food is better than that of restaurants in the city.[[434]](#cite_note-Street_food_of_Mumbai_nyt09-437)[[435]](#cite_note-Street_food_of_Mumbai_td10-438)[[436]](#cite_note-Street_food_of_Mumbai_dnaj09a-439) People of Mumbai, cutting across barriers of class, religion, gender, and ethnicity, are passionate about street food.[[437]](#cite_note-Street_food_of_Mumbai_jsa08-440) Street food vendors are credited by some for developing the city's [food culture](/wiki/Food_culture "Food culture").[[438]](#cite_note-Street_food_of_Mumbai_dc11-441) Street food in Mumbai is relatively inexpensive as compared to restaurants, and vendors tend to be clustered around crowded areas such as colleges and railway stations.[[434]](#cite_note-Street_food_of_Mumbai_nyt09-437)

## Media

See also: [List of Mumbai radio stations](/wiki/List_of_Mumbai_radio_stations "List of Mumbai radio stations")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Film_City.jpg/250px-Film_City.jpg)](/wiki/File:Film_City.jpg)

Plaque at [Film City](/wiki/Film_City,_Mumbai "Film City, Mumbai") entrance

[Bollywood](/wiki/Bollywood "Bollywood"), the Hindi film industry based in Mumbai, produces around 150–200 films every year.[[439]](#cite_note-442) The name Bollywood is a blend of Bombay and [Hollywood](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_States "Cinema of the United States").[[440]](#cite_note-443) The 2000s saw a growth in Bollywood's popularity overseas. This led filmmaking to new heights in terms of quality, cinematography and innovative story lines as well as technical advances such as special effects and animation.[[441]](#cite_note-444) Studios in Goregaon, including [Film City](/wiki/Film_City,_Mumbai "Film City, Mumbai"), are the location for most movie sets.[[442]](#cite_note-445)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Times_of_India_Building.jpg/250px-Times_of_India_Building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Times_of_India_Building.jpg)

*[The Times of India](/wiki/The_Times_of_India "The Times of India")*'s first office is opposite the [Chhatrapati Shivaji Terminus](/wiki/Chhatrapati_Shivaji_Terminus "Chhatrapati Shivaji Terminus") where it was founded.[[443]](#cite_note-televisionpoint2006-446)

Mumbai has numerous [newspaper](/wiki/Indian_newspapers "Indian newspapers") publications, television and radio stations. Marathi dailies enjoy the maximum readership share in the city and the top [Marathi language newspapers](/wiki/Marathi-language_newspapers "Marathi-language newspapers") are *[Maharashtra Times](/wiki/Maharashtra_Times "Maharashtra Times")*, *[Navakaal](/wiki/Nava_Kaal "Nava Kaal")*, *[Lokmat](/wiki/Lokmat "Lokmat")*, *[Loksatta](/wiki/Loksatta_(newspaper) "Loksatta (newspaper)")*, *Mumbai Chaufer*, *[Saamana](/wiki/Saamana "Saamana")* and *[Sakaal](/wiki/Sakaal "Sakaal")*.[[444]](#cite_note-447) English language newspapers published in Mumbai include *[The Times of India](/wiki/The_Times_of_India "The Times of India")*, *[Hindustan Times](/wiki/Hindustan_Times "Hindustan Times")*, and *[The Indian Express](/wiki/The_Indian_Express "The Indian Express")*.[[445]](#cite_note-448) Asia's oldest newspaper, *[Bombay Samachar](/wiki/Bombay_Samachar "Bombay Samachar")*, has been published in Gujarati since 1822.[[446]](#cite_note-449) *Bombay Durpan*, the first Marathi newspaper, was founded by [Balshastri Jambhekar](/wiki/Balshastri_Jambhekar "Balshastri Jambhekar") in 1832.[[447]](#cite_note-PubliclifeMSG-450)

The metropolis is the hub of many international media corporations, with many news channels and print publications. The national television broadcaster, [Doordarshan](/wiki/Doordarshan "Doordarshan"), provides two free terrestrial channels, while three main cable networks serve most households.[[448]](#cite_note-451)[[449]](#cite_note-452) Prominent DTH entertainment services in Mumbai include [Dish TV](/wiki/Dish_TV "Dish TV") and [Tata Sky](/wiki/Tata_Sky "Tata Sky").[[450]](#cite_note-453)

There are twelve radio stations in Mumbai, with nine broadcasting on the [FM](/wiki/Frequency_modulation "Frequency modulation") band, and three [All India Radio](/wiki/All_India_Radio "All India Radio") stations broadcasting on the [AM](/wiki/Amplitude_modulation "Amplitude modulation") band.[[451]](#cite_note-454) Mumbai has access to [Commercial radio](/wiki/Commercial_broadcasting "Commercial broadcasting") providers such as [Sirius](/wiki/Sirius_Satellite_Radio "Sirius Satellite Radio"). The [Conditional Access System](/wiki/Conditional_Access_System "Conditional Access System") started by the [Union Government](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India") in 2006 met a poor response in Mumbai due to competition from its sister technology [Direct-to-Home](/wiki/Direct_broadcast_satellite "Direct broadcast satellite") transmission service.[[452]](#cite_note-455)

## Sports

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Wankhede_Stadium_%2886312941%29.jpeg/250px-Wankhede_Stadium_%2886312941%29.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Wankhede_Stadium_(86312941).jpeg)

[Wankhede Stadium](/wiki/Wankhede_Stadium "Wankhede Stadium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Wug0reym7cv91.jpg/250px-Wug0reym7cv91.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wug0reym7cv91.jpg)

[Mumbai Football Arena](/wiki/Mumbai_Football_Arena "Mumbai Football Arena")

[Cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") is more popular than any other sport in Mumbai. It is home to the [Board of Control for Cricket in India](/wiki/Board_of_Control_for_Cricket_in_India "Board of Control for Cricket in India") (BCCI)[[453]](#cite_note-456) and [Indian Premier League](/wiki/Indian_Premier_League "Indian Premier League") (IPL).[[454]](#cite_note-457) Mumbai's [first-class](/wiki/First-class_cricket "First-class cricket") team [Mumbai cricket team](/wiki/Mumbai_cricket_team "Mumbai cricket team") has won 41 [Ranji Trophy](/wiki/Ranji_Trophy "Ranji Trophy") titles, the most by any team.[[455]](#cite_note-458) The city based [Mumbai Indians](/wiki/Mumbai_Indians "Mumbai Indians") compete in the Indian Premier League. Mumbai has two international cricket stadiums, the [Wankhede Stadium](/wiki/Wankhede_Stadium "Wankhede Stadium") and the [Brabourne Stadium](/wiki/Brabourne_Stadium "Brabourne Stadium"). The first cricket test match in India was played in Mumbai at the [Bombay Gymkhana](/wiki/Bombay_Gymkhana "Bombay Gymkhana").[[456]](#cite_note-459)[[457]](#cite_note-460) The biggest cricketing event to be staged in the city so far is the [final](/wiki/2011_Cricket_World_Cup_Final "2011 Cricket World Cup Final") of the [2011 ICC Cricket World Cup](/wiki/2011_ICC_Cricket_World_Cup "2011 ICC Cricket World Cup") which was played at the Wankhede Stadium. Mumbai and [London](/wiki/London "London") are the only two cities to have hosted both a World Cup final and the [final](/wiki/2006_ICC_Champions_Trophy_Final "2006 ICC Champions Trophy Final") of an [ICC Champions Trophy](/wiki/ICC_Champions_Trophy "ICC Champions Trophy") which was played at the [Brabourne Stadium](/wiki/Brabourne_Stadium "Brabourne Stadium") in [2006](/wiki/2006_ICC_Champions_Trophy "2006 ICC Champions Trophy").[[458]](#cite_note-TSMH-461)

[Football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") is another popular sport in the city, with the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") and the English [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") being followed widely.[[459]](#cite_note-462) The [Mumbai City FC](/wiki/Mumbai_City_FC "Mumbai City FC") of [Indian Super League](/wiki/Indian_Super_League "Indian Super League") (ISL) play their home matches at the [Mumbai Football Arena](/wiki/Mumbai_Football_Arena "Mumbai Football Arena").[[460]](#cite_note-463) While the [I-League 2](/wiki/I-League_2 "I-League 2") club [Mumbai Kenkre FC](/wiki/Mumbai_Kenkre_FC "Mumbai Kenkre FC") uses the [Cooperage Ground](/wiki/Cooperage_Ground "Cooperage Ground") as home ground.[[461]](#cite_note-464)

Mumbai's first professional [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football") [franchise](/wiki/Franchise_(sports) "Franchise (sports)"),[[462]](#cite_note-465) the [Mumbai Gladiators](/wiki/Mumbai_Gladiators "Mumbai Gladiators"), played its first season, in [Pune](/wiki/Pune "Pune"), in late 2012.[[463]](#cite_note-466)

In [field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey"), Mumbai is home to the [Mumbai Marines](/wiki/Mumbai_Marines "Mumbai Marines") and [Mumbai Magicians](/wiki/Mumbai_Magicians "Mumbai Magicians") in the [World Series Hockey](/wiki/World_Series_Hockey "World Series Hockey") and [Hockey India League](/wiki/Hockey_India_League "Hockey India League") respectively. Matches in the city are played at the [Mahindra Hockey Stadium](/wiki/Mahindra_Hockey_Stadium "Mahindra Hockey Stadium").[[464]](#cite_note-marines-467)[[465]](#cite_note-magicians-468)

The Indian Badminton League (IBL), now known as the [Premier Badminton League](/wiki/Premier_Badminton_League "Premier Badminton League") is also visiting Mumbai since its inaugural edition in 2013 when the final was held in Mumbai's [National Sports Club of India](/wiki/National_Sports_Club_of_India "National Sports Club of India").[[466]](#cite_note-469)

[Rugby](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") is another growing sport in Mumbai with league matches being held at the [Bombay Gymkhana](/wiki/Bombay_Gymkhana "Bombay Gymkhana") from June to November.[[467]](#cite_note-rugbyhost-470)

Every February, Mumbai holds [derby](/wiki/Derby_(horse_race) "Derby (horse race)") races at the [Mahalaxmi Racecourse](/wiki/Mahalaxmi_Racecourse "Mahalaxmi Racecourse"). [Mcdowell's](/wiki/United_Breweries_Group "United Breweries Group") Derby is held in February at the [Turf Club](/wiki/Royal_Western_India_Turf_Club "Royal Western India Turf Club") in Mumbai.[[468]](#cite_note-471) In March 2004, the Mumbai Grand Prix was part of the [F1 powerboat world championship](/wiki/F1_Powerboat_Racing "F1 Powerboat Racing"),[[469]](#cite_note-472) and the [Force India](/wiki/Force_India "Force India") F1 team car was unveiled in the city, in 2008.[[470]](#cite_note-473) In 2004, the annual [Mumbai Marathon](/wiki/Mumbai_Marathon "Mumbai Marathon") was established as a part of "[The Greatest Race on Earth](/wiki/The_Greatest_Race_on_Earth "The Greatest Race on Earth")". Mumbai had also played host to the [Kingfisher Airlines Tennis Open](/wiki/Kingfisher_Airlines_Tennis_Open "Kingfisher Airlines Tennis Open"), an [International Series](/wiki/International_Series_Tournaments "International Series Tournaments") tournament of the [ATP World Tour](/wiki/Association_of_Tennis_Professionals "Association of Tennis Professionals"), in 2006 and 2007.[[471]](#cite_note-474)

Mumbai hosted the [141st IOC Session](/wiki/141st_IOC_Session "141st IOC Session") from 15 to 17 October 2023.[[472]](#cite_note-475)
